The AI-Evals Research Landscape: A Cartographer's Map

The evaluation literature is not a flat list of benchmarks—it is a lineage. Each generation of eval research answers a failure of the previous one. What follows traces that line: classic NLP metrics → benchmarks → RLHF/preferences → LLM-as-judge → agent & RL-environment evals → safety/adversarial, and the through-lines that stitch these clusters together.

1. Classic NLP metrics: the problem of automatic scoring

Before there were benchmarks, there was the question of how to score free-form text at all. This cluster is small but foundational, and its anxieties echo through everything downstream.

  • How NOT To Evaluate Your Dialogue System (2016) — 1603.08023 — n-gram overlap metrics (BLEU, ROUGE) correlate weakly with human judgment on open-ended generation. The original indictment of cheap automatic metrics.
  • BERTScore (2019) — 1904.09675 — replace surface overlap with contextual-embedding similarity. The bridge from lexical to learned evaluation.

The unresolved tension here—surface metrics are gameable and human eval doesn't scale—is the seed that later germinates into both reward modeling and LLM-as-judge.

2. Benchmarks & integrity: the leaderboard era

If you can't score one output well, score thousands and aggregate. This is the largest cluster (392 nodes) and the field's center of gravity.

  • GLUE (2018) — 1804.07461 — bundle diverse NLU tasks into one multi-task scoreboard. Defined the modern benchmark format.
  • SuperGLUE (2019) — 1905.00537 — when models saturate GLUE, raise the difficulty. The first explicit saturation-driven benchmark refresh.
  • DROP (2019) — 1903.00161 — demand discrete reasoning (counting, arithmetic) that pattern-matching can't fake.
  • Evaluating LLMs Trained on Code / HumanEval (2021) — 2107.03374 — execution-based scoring (does the code run and pass tests?). The single highest-pull node (in-deg 104); functional correctness is gaming-resistant in a way text overlap never was.
  • GPQA (2023) — 2311.12022 — "Google-proof" graduate questions to outrun both saturation and contamination.
  • LiveCodeBench (2024) — 2403.07974 — contamination-free by construction via time-windowed problems.

The internal evolution of this cluster is itself a through-line: saturation → harder benchmarks → contamination concerns → integrity-by-construction.

3. RLHF & preferences: optimizing toward human judgment

Benchmarks measure; preferences let you optimize. This cluster (and its sibling, reward models) reframes evaluation as a training signal.

  • Deep RL from Human Preferences (2017) — 1706.03741 — learn a reward model from pairwise human comparisons. The keystone (in-deg 50); evaluation becomes the objective.
  • Fine-Tuning LMs from Human Preferences (2019) — 1909.08593 — port that recipe to language models.
  • Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant (2022) — 2204.05862 — the HH framing that operationalized "good output" for assistants.
  • DPO (2023) — 2305.18290 — your LM is secretly a reward model; skip the separate RL loop. Collapsed the pipeline.
  • Scalable agent alignment via reward modeling (2018) — 1811.07871 — the research agenda naming reward modeling as the scalable-oversight primitive.

The reward model is the pivot of the entire map: it is simultaneously an evaluator (cluster 1/4), a training signal (this cluster), and—crucially—an attack surface (cluster 6).

4. LLM-as-judge: models grading models

Human preference labels don't scale to every dev-loop iteration. The fix: use a strong LLM as the evaluator. This cluster (161 nodes) is the direct heir to both the "metrics don't scale" problem and the reward-model insight.

  • MT-Bench / Chatbot Arena (2023) — 2306.05685 — validate that GPT-4 judgments and crowd Elo track human preference. The cluster's anchor (in-deg 44, 9k+ citations).
  • G-Eval (2023) — 2303.16634 — GPT-4 with chain-of-thought as an NLG scorer, closing the loop opened by How NOT To Evaluate eight years earlier.
  • Prometheus (2023) — 2310.08491 — an open, fine-tuned judge with explicit rubrics, so evaluation isn't locked behind a proprietary API.
  • RewardBench (2024) — 2403.13787 — a benchmark for the judges themselves—reflexively closing clusters 2 and 3.

This is where the lineage becomes a loop: benchmarks measure judges, judges score preference data, preference data trains reward models, reward models are themselves benchmarked.

5. Agent & RL-environment evals: scoring behavior, not strings

Once models act over many steps, single-output scoring breaks. Evaluation moves into environments with task completion as the metric—inheriting directly from the classical RL evaluation tradition.

  • The Arcade Learning Environment (2012) — 1207.4708 — the ancestral idea: a standardized environment suite as a general-agent benchmark.
  • WebArena (2023) — 2307.13854 — realistic, self-hosted web tasks with execution-based success.
  • SWE-bench (2023) — 2310.06770 — resolve real GitHub issues, graded by the repo's own test suite. The purest fusion of cluster-2 execution scoring with agentic action.
  • GAIA (2023) — 2311.12983 — assistant tasks easy for humans, hard for models—a deliberate capability-gap probe.
  • τ-bench (2024) — 2406.12045 — tool-agent-user interaction, scoring multi-turn reliability and policy adherence.

Note the through-line back to cluster 2: HumanEval's "run the tests" logic is SWE-bench's grader. Execution-based evaluation is the connective tissue from code benchmarks to agent benchmarks.

6. Safety & adversarial: where the metric breaks

The closing cluster studies how every evaluator above fails—through artifacts, attacks, and over-optimization.

  • Annotation Artifacts in NLI (2018) — 1803.02324 — benchmarks contain spurious shortcuts; high scores can be hollow.
  • Adversarial Examples for Reading Comprehension (2017) — 1707.07328 — distractor sentences collapse "solved" tasks.
  • Scaling Laws for Reward Model Over-optimization (2022) — 2210.10760 — push hard on a proxy reward and true quality degrades. Reward hacking, quantified.
  • Large Language Models are not Fair Evaluators (2023) — 2305.17926 — judge models carry position and verbosity biases; the cluster-4 shortcut is itself gameable.
  • Red Teaming LMs with LMs (2022) — 2202.03286 — automate adversarial probing to scale safety evaluation.

The through-lines

Three currents run across all six clusters:

  1. The reward model is the hub. Born in RLHF (cluster 3), it powers verifiers/judges (cluster 4), supplies the success signal for agents (cluster 5), and is the thing that gets hacked (cluster 6). Trace any cross-cluster edge and it usually passes through a learned reward.

  2. Execution beats overlap. The arc from BLEU's failure (cluster 1) → HumanEval's test-passing (cluster 2) → SWE-bench's repo tests (cluster 5) is one idea repeated at rising stakes: ground evaluation in verifiable outcomes, not string similarity.

  3. Every evaluator becomes a target. Artifacts undermine benchmarks, contamination undermines leaderboards, bias undermines judges, over-optimization undermines reward models. Cluster 6 is the immune response to clusters 2–5.

The genuinely-recent frontier

  • Benchmark integrity by construction — LiveCodeBench (2024) and GPQA (2023) treat contamination and saturation as first-class design constraints, not afterthoughts.
  • Reward hacking as a measured phenomenon — Scaling Laws for Reward Model Over-optimization (2022) turned a folk worry into a quantified failure mode now central to alignment.
  • Agent reliability over single-shot capability — τ-bench (2024) and WebArena (2023) shift the question from "can it answer?" to "can it complete a multi-turn task while obeying constraints?"—the open problem the field is now racing toward.
  • Reflexive evaluation — RewardBench (2024) benchmarking the judges marks the field turning its instruments on itself, the clearest sign the loop has fully closed.

The map, in one line: the field keeps inventing cheaper evaluators, discovering they're gameable, and building the next cluster to catch the leak.

  • AGIEval: A Human-Centric Benchmark for Evaluating Foundation Models (2023) — in-deg 14, cites 847, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.06364
  • Latent Retrieval for Weakly Supervised Open Domain Question Answering (2019) — in-deg 14, cites 1162, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/1906.00300
  • Reflexion: language agents with verbal reinforcement learning (2023) — in-deg 13, cites 3929, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2303.11366
  • Did Aristotle Use a Laptop? A Question Answering Benchmark with Implicit Reasoning Strategies (2021) — in-deg 13, cites 1073, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2101.02235
  • Calibrate Before Use: Improving Few-Shot Performance of Language Models (2021) — in-deg 13, cites 1918, depth 2 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.09690
  • LiveCodeBench: Holistic and Contamination Free Evaluation of Large Language Models for Code (2024) — in-deg 12, cites 1691, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.07974
