  • "We present Sparrow, an information-seeking dialogue agent trained to be more helpful, correct, and harmless compared to prompted language model baselines." — https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.14375
  • "we break down the requirements for good dialogue into natural language rules the agent should follow, and ask raters about each rule separately. We demonstrate that this breakdown enables us to collect more targeted human judgements of agent behaviour and allows for more efficient rule-conditional reward models." — https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.14375
  • "For factual questions, evidence provided by Sparrow supports the sampled response 78% of the time. Sparrow is preferred more often than baselines while being more resilient to adversarial probing by humans, violating our rules only 8% of the time when probed." — https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.14375
  • "Finally, we conduct extensive analyses showing that though our model learns to follow our rules it can exhibit distributional biases." — https://arxiv.org/abs/2209.14375

Why it matters for agent evals

Sparrow seeds several patterns now central to agent evaluation. (1) Rule-decomposed judging: instead of one holistic score, evaluate behavior against an explicit checklist of rules, each scored independently — the direct ancestor of rubric-based and per-criterion LLM-as-judge / verifier setups and of safety taxonomies used in red-team scoring. (2) Rule-conditional reward models are early trained verifiers: classifiers that judge whether a specific constraint was violated, the building block for automated graders in RL environments and eval harnesses. (3) Adversarial human probing establishes break-rate-under-attack (here 8%) as a first-class safety/adversarial metric, formalizing red-teaming as evaluation rather than anecdote. (4) Evidence/source grounding ties factual correctness to verifiable retrieval (78% support rate), prefiguring citation-faithfulness and attribution evals for retrieval-augmented agents. (5) The distributional-bias caveat is a durable evaluation lesson: passing an enumerated rule set does not certify fairness, so benchmarks must separately probe distributional harms. Together these make the paper a reference point connecting judges, verifiers, RLHF reward design, and adversarial safety evals.

Themes

1 why-evals · 2 eval⇄capability⇄RL-env · 8 judge/verifiers · 10 safety/adversarial · 9 agent-specific

Notes — "SuperGLUE: A Stickier Benchmark for General-Purpose Language Understanding Systems"

Authors: Alex Wang, Yada Pruksachatkun, Nikita Nangia, Amanpreet Singh, Julian Michael, Felix Hill, Omer Levy, Samuel R. Bowman · Venue/Year: NeurIPS 2019 · URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.00537 · Type: paper · Found: true

Summary

SuperGLUE is the direct successor to GLUE, built because within a year of GLUE's release pretraining (notably BERT) pushed model performance past the non-expert human baseline, leaving little headroom. The authors assembled a harder, more diverse suite of eight English language-understanding tasks, retained GLUE's single-number leaderboard format, and shipped a software toolkit plus a public leaderboard at super.gluebenchmark.com. They establish a strong BERT-based baseline (~69) that still trails a measured human baseline (~89.8) by nearly 20 points, restoring meaningful research headroom. SuperGLUE became foundational because it codified a reusable recipe for building "stickier" benchmarks — explicit selection criteria, human baselines as a saturation tripwire, and format diversity beyond simple sentence-pair classification — and served as the standard NLU yardstick during the GPT-3 / T5 era. Its rapid eventual saturation also became a canonical cautionary tale about benchmark lifespan in the LLM era.

Key points

  • Motivation: GLUE saturated fast — "performance on the benchmark has recently surpassed the level of non-expert humans, suggesting limited headroom for further research."
  • Composition: eight tasks — BoolQ (yes/no QA), CB (3-class NLI), COPA (2-choice causal reasoning), MultiRC (multi-answer reading comprehension), ReCoRD (cloze-style coreference QA), RTE (2-class entailment), WiC (word-sense disambiguation), WSC (Winograd pronoun coreference).
  • Retains the two hardest GLUE tasks (RTE, WSC); the rest came from an open public call for task proposals, then filtered by criteria.
  • Six explicit selection criteria: task substance (reasoning over English), difficulty (beyond SOTA but solvable by college-educated speakers), evaluability (automatic metric ~ human judgment), public data with usable licenses, simple unified formats, and format diversity (longer contexts, not just sentence/sentence-pair classification).
  • Introduces format diversity deliberately to discourage task-specific architectural hacks and push toward general-purpose models.
  • Headline numbers: best BERT-based baseline (BERT++) ≈ 69.0 overall vs. human baseline ≈ 89.8 — a ~20-point gap by design.
  • Ships not just data but a software toolkit (built on jiant) and a public leaderboard with a single summary score, lowering the cost of standardized evaluation.
  • Human baselines are collected per-task and aggregated, making "distance to human" a first-class, comparable metric across heterogeneous tasks.
  • Includes diagnostic/analysis sets (e.g., a broad-coverage diagnostic and Winogender for gender-bias measurement) alongside the core scored tasks.
  • Established the template later inherited by harder successors and informed the broader "benchmarks keep saturating" critique that motivated dynamic and adversarial evaluation.

Verified quotes

  • "performance on the benchmark has recently surpassed the level of non-expert humans, suggesting limited headroom for further research." — https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.00537
  • "In this paper we present SuperGLUE, a new benchmark styled after GLUE with a new set of more difficult language understanding tasks, a software toolkit, and a public leaderboard." — https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.00537
  • "Tasks should be beyond the scope of current state-of-the-art systems, but solvable by most college-educated English speakers." — https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.00537 (ar5iv full text)
  • "On average, there is a nearly 20 point gap between BERT++ and human performance." — https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.00537 (ar5iv full text)

Why it matters for agent evals

SuperGLUE encodes two ideas that remain central to evaluating capable agents. First, the human baseline as a saturation tripwire: it operationalizes "is this benchmark still measuring anything?" by anchoring scores to measured human performance and tracking the gap — the same diagnostic now applied to agent benchmarks that risk ceiling-out. Second, the explicit design-criteria recipe (difficulty calibrated above SOTA but human-solvable, format diversity to resist architecture-specific gaming, automatic metrics validated against human judgment) is a reusable spec for constructing trustworthy eval suites, including the verifiable-task and RL-environment design that agent training depends on. Its single-number aggregate leaderboard plus open toolkit prefigured standardized harnesses (lm-eval-harness, HELM). Equally instructive for eval integrity: SuperGLUE was itself saturated within roughly a year of release by large models, making it the canonical example of why static, contamination-prone benchmarks have short shelf lives and why agent evals trend toward dynamic, adversarial, and held-out-verifier designs.

Themes

1 why-evals · 6 benchmark-vs-eval/integrity · 5 eval infra

Notes — "Training a Helpful and Harmless Assistant with Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback"

Authors: Yuntao Bai, Andy Jones, Kamal Ndousse, Amanda Askell, Anna Chen, Nova DasSarma, Dawn Drain, Stanislav Fort, Deep Ganguli, Tom Henighan, ... Dario Amodei, Tom Brown, Jack Clark, Sam McCandlish, Chris Olah, Ben Mann, Jared Kaplan (Anthropic) · Venue/Year: arXiv preprint, 2022 · URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.05862 · Type: paper · Found: true

Summary

This is Anthropic's foundational RLHF paper: it shows how to fine-tune a language model into a "helpful and harmless" conversational assistant using preference modeling plus reinforcement learning from human feedback. The work establishes the now-canonical pipeline — collect pairwise human comparisons of model responses, train a preference (reward) model, then optimize the policy with RL against that reward subject to a KL penalty toward the initial model. It became heavily cited because it is the most thorough early public account of RLHF applied to open-ended dialogue (as opposed to InstructGPT's instruction following or earlier summarization work), and because it introduced durable concepts: the helpfulness/harmlessness split, separate red-teaming data collection, iterated online RLHF, the "alignment tax," and a clean empirical scaling relation between RL reward and the square root of KL divergence. Much of the subsequent alignment and eval literature (Constitutional AI, instruction-tuned chatbots, reward-model evaluation, LLM-as-judge) builds directly on its data-collection and evaluation framing.

Key points

  • Method. Preference modeling + RLHF: humans compare two model responses in a dialogue, those comparisons train a preference model (reward model), and a policy is RL-fine-tuned against the PM with a KL penalty to its initialization (PPO-style).
  • Two axes, two data distributions. Helpfulness and harmlessness are collected separately — crowdworkers elicit helpful responses in one interface and adversarially red-team for harmful responses in another — surfacing a real helpfulness vs. harmlessness tension the PM must balance.
  • Alignment improves capability, not just safety. The abstract states alignment training "improves performance on almost all NLP evaluations" — i.e. an alignment bonus at large scale rather than a pure tax; the small alignment tax that exists shrinks with model size.
  • Compatible with specialized skills. RLHF alignment is "fully compatible with training for specialized skills such as python coding and summarization" — alignment and capability training compose.
  • Iterated online RLHF introduced. Preference models and RL policies are refreshed on a weekly cadence with fresh human feedback, continuously improving both datasets and models — an early account of online/continual preference data flywheels.
  • Robustness / scaling law. A roughly linear relation between RL reward and the square root of the KL divergence between policy and initialization — a practical diagnostic for how far RL has pushed the policy and when reward is being over-optimized.
  • Scale. Models span roughly 10M to 52B parameters; preference-model performance and the helpful/harmless balance improve with scale.
  • Evaluation via Elo / pairwise human preference. Models are compared with human pairwise judgments aggregated into Elo-style scores, plus standard NLP/zero-shot benchmarks — pairing crowd preference evals with capability benchmarks.
  • Released a public human-preference dataset of helpfulness and harmlessness comparisons that became a standard reward-modeling / RLHF research resource.

Verified quotes

  • "We apply preference modeling and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to finetune language models to act as helpful and harmless assistants." — https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.05862
  • "We find this alignment training improves performance on almost all NLP evaluations, and is fully compatible with training for specialized skills such as python coding and summarization." — https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.05862
  • "We explore an iterated online mode of training, where preference models and RL policies are updated on a weekly cadence with fresh human feedback data, efficiently improving our datasets and models." — https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.05862
  • "we ... identify a roughly linear relation between the RL reward and the square root of the KL divergence between the policy and its initialization." — https://arxiv.org/abs/2204.05862

Why it matters for agent evals

This paper seeds several primitives that the agent-eval stack still uses. (1) Reward/preference models as automated judges: the trained PM is an early "judge" predicting human preference over candidate responses — the conceptual ancestor of LLM-as-judge and learned verifiers used to score agent trajectories. (2) RL environment design for language: it operationalizes a language RL loop (reward model as the environment's reward signal, KL-to-init as a regularizer), and the reward-vs-√KL relation is a concrete reward-hacking/over-optimization diagnostic relevant to anyone running RL on eval-derived rewards. (3) Pairwise/Elo human preference as an eval methodology — complementary to fixed benchmarks — plus the explicit alignment-tax framing for measuring whether safety training degrades capability. (4) Red-teaming as a distinct evaluation distribution for harmlessness, foundational to adversarial/safety evals. (5) The released human-preference dataset became a standard testbed for evaluating reward models and verifiers themselves.

Themes

2 eval⇄capability⇄RL-env · 7 RL environments · 8 judge/verifiers · 10 safety/adversarial · 1 why-evals

Notes — "Training Verifiers to Solve Math Word Problems"

Authors: Karl Cobbe, Vineet Kosaraju, Mohammad Bavarian, Mark Chen, Heewoo Jun, Lukasz Kaiser, Matthias Plappert, Jerry Tworek, Jacob Hilton, Reiichiro Nakano, Christopher Hesse, John Schulman (OpenAI) · Venue/Year: arXiv preprint, 2021 · URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.14168 · Type: paper · Found: true

Summary

This OpenAI paper introduces GSM8K, a dataset of 8.5K linguistically diverse grade-school math word problems, built specifically to diagnose the multi-step reasoning failures of large language models. The headline methodological contribution is training verifiers: instead of relying on a single finetuned generator, they sample many candidate solutions at test time and use a learned verifier model to judge correctness and select the best one. They show this verification-plus-sampling approach substantially outperforms finetuning a generator of equal size, and — crucially — that it scales more effectively with additional data than the finetuning baseline. The paper became foundational because it crystallized the generator/verifier split, established GSM8K as the default benchmark for LLM arithmetic reasoning, and provided the conceptual seed for later work on outcome- and process-reward models, best-of-N selection, and verifier-guided RL.

Key points

  • Introduces GSM8K: 8.5K high-quality, linguistically diverse grade-school math word problems (split ~7.5K train / 1K test), each requiring 2–8 steps of elementary arithmetic — conceptually simple but multi-step.
  • Core finding: even the largest transformer models fail to achieve high test performance despite the conceptual simplicity, exposing a robustness gap in multi-step reasoning.
  • Method — verification: train a separate verifier to score the correctness of full model completions; at test time sample many candidates and select the one the verifier ranks highest (a best-of-N / reranking scheme).
  • Verifiers are trained on a dataset of model-generated solutions labeled by whether they reached the correct final answer (outcome-based correctness signal).
  • Verification beats finetuning at the same model scale, and the gap widens with more data — i.e., verification has a better scaling trend than simply finetuning a larger generator.
  • Token-level (per-step) verifier scoring outperformed solution-level scoring, foreshadowing later process-reward-model work.
  • Establishes the generator + verifier decomposition that decouples producing candidate reasoning from judging it — a now-standard pattern in reasoning systems.
  • GSM8K became one of the most widely adopted reasoning benchmarks, used to evaluate chain-of-thought, self-consistency, tool use, and later frontier reasoning models.
  • Demonstrates that test-time compute (sampling more candidates and reranking) is a lever for accuracy, not just training scale.

Verified quotes

  • "we introduce GSM8K, a dataset of 8.5K high quality linguistically diverse grade school math word problems." — https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.14168
  • "To increase performance, we propose training verifiers to judge the correctness of model completions. At test time, we generate many candidate solutions and select the one ranked highest by the verifier." — https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.14168
  • "We demonstrate that verification significantly improves performance on GSM8K, and we provide strong empirical evidence that verification scales more effectively with increased data than a finetuning baseline." — https://arxiv.org/abs/2110.14168

Why it matters for agent evals

This paper is a direct ancestor of the verifier / reward-model line that underpins modern eval and RL infrastructure. The generator+verifier split is the conceptual template for LLM-as-judge and learned verifiers: a model that scores candidate trajectories rather than producing them. The outcome-supervised verifier here is the precursor to outcome reward models (ORM) and process reward models (PRM) used in RLHF/RLVR pipelines, and GSM8K became a canonical RL-environment / verifiable-reward benchmark — math problems have checkable final answers, making them ideal for automated grading without human judges. The best-of-N reranking insight (spend test-time compute, then verify-and-select) is the foundation for self-consistency, tree search, and verifier-guided decoding in agentic systems. For agent evals specifically, it seeds the principle that verification is often easier and more scalable than generation, motivating eval designs where a trusted checker grades an agent's outputs, and it gave the field a clean, automatically-gradable benchmark that anchored years of reasoning-capability measurement.

Themes

  • 2 eval⇄capability⇄RL-env — GSM8K is a verifiable-reward environment that ties a capability (multi-step reasoning) to an automatically-gradable eval/RL signal.
  • 6 benchmark-vs-eval/integrity — introduces a foundational, widely-cited benchmark (with the attendant saturation/contamination concerns it later raised).
  • 8 judge/verifiers — the central contribution is a trained verifier that judges completion correctness, the direct ancestor of ORM/PRM and LLM-as-judge.

Notes — "Reinforcement Learning on Web Interfaces Using Workflow-Guided Exploration"

Authors: Evan Zheran Liu, Kelvin Guu, Panupong Pasupat, Tianlin Shi, Percy Liang (Stanford) · Venue/Year: ICLR 2018 · URL: https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.08802 · Type: paper · Found: true

Summary

This paper tackles the sparse-reward exploration problem for RL agents acting on real web UIs — booking flights, replying to emails, filling forms — where a single wrong action ruins the whole episode and the agent almost never stumbles onto reward by random exploration. Instead of the usual fix of "warm-starting" via behavioral cloning on demonstrations (which overfits), the authors use demonstrations to constrain exploration: from each demo they induce high-level "workflows" (abstracted action templates like "Step 1: click a textbox; Step 2: type text") that restrict the action set at each step to those resembling the demonstration. An exploration policy learns which workflows lead to reward and samples actions that satisfy them, pruning bad directions and accelerating reward discovery. They pair this with a new neural policy (DOMNET) built for the semi-structured DOM-tree nature of websites, and evaluate on the World of Bits / MiniWoB benchmark, reporting new state-of-the-art and >100x sample-efficiency gains over behavioral cloning. It became heavily cited as one of the foundational works for web/GUI agents — it popularized MiniWoB as an agent benchmark and seeded the demonstration-guided-RL and DOM-aware-policy lines that later web-agent and computer-use research builds on.

Key points

  • Problem framing: deep RL on web tasks stalls because reward is sparse and a single early mistake ("ruin the entire sequence of actions") makes successful trajectories essentially undiscoverable by trial-and-error.
  • Core idea — demonstrations constrain exploration, not just imitation. Rather than cloning demos (prone to overfitting), they induce high-level workflows from demos and use them to prune the action space during exploration. This is a meaningfully different use of demonstrations than warm-starting / behavioral cloning.
  • Workflows = abstracted action templates. Each demonstration yields workflows that specify the kind of action allowed per step (e.g., "click a textbox," "enter some text") rather than the exact action, letting the agent generalize across instances of a task while staying near the demonstrated path.
  • Workflow lattice + exploration policy. Demos induce a lattice of candidate workflows; an exploration policy learns to identify which workflows actually yield reward and samples reward-satisfying actions from them — the workflows "prune out bad exploration directions and accelerate the agent's ability to discover rewards."
  • DOMNET — a DOM-aware neural policy. A novel policy architecture designed for the semi-structured (DOM-tree, element-attribute) nature of web pages, as opposed to pixel-only or flat-vector policies.
  • Benchmark: evaluated on a suite of web tasks including the then-recent World of Bits / MiniWoB benchmark of browser micro-tasks.
  • Headline result — sample efficiency: workflow-guided exploration "improves sample efficiency over behavioral cloning by more than 100x," and achieves new state-of-the-art on the web-task suite.
  • Reward-shaping-free leverage: the method gets its gains from constraining where the agent explores rather than from hand-engineered reward shaping, making it portable across tasks with the same sparse-reward structure.
  • What it introduced: a reusable recipe — demonstration-induced action constraints for sparse-reward RL — plus a DOM-structured policy and an early, influential use of MiniWoB as a web-agent RL environment.

Verified quotes

  • "From each demonstration, we induce high-level "workflows" which constrain the allowable actions at each time step to be similar to those in the demonstration (e.g., "Step 1: click on a textbox; Step 2: enter some text")." — https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.08802
  • "Workflows prune out bad exploration directions and accelerate the agent's ability to discover rewards." — https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.08802
  • "We achieve new state-of-the-art results, and show that workflow-guided exploration improves sample efficiency over behavioral cloning by more than 100x." — https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.08802
  • "This has been a notable problem in training deep RL agents to perform web-based tasks, such as booking flights or replying to emails, where a single mistake can ruin the entire sequence of actions." — https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.08802

Why it matters for agent evals

This is a load-bearing ancestor of the modern web/GUI-agent eval stack. It is one of the works that established MiniWoB / World of Bits as an RL environment and benchmark for browser-acting agents — the lineage that runs through MiniWoB++, WebShop, WebArena, and computer-use evals. For agent-evals specifically it does three things: (1) it cleanly names the sparse-reward, long-horizon, irrecoverable-mistake structure that still defines why web-agent tasks are hard to both train and score — a single early error tanks the whole trajectory, which is exactly the brittleness later reliability evals (e.g., pass^k, long-horizon reliability work) try to measure. (2) It demonstrates that the bottleneck is exploration/credit-assignment in a sparse binary-reward environment, motivating the dense verifiers, intermediate checkpoints, and shaped rewards that today's RL-environment and verifier-design work provides. (3) It models a website as a semi-structured DOM observation/action space, anticipating the DOM/accessibility-tree representations that current web-agent harnesses and benchmarks expose to agents and judges. In short, it sits at the eval ⇄ capability ⇄ RL-environment junction: the same task suite serves as both the training environment and the evaluation benchmark, foreshadowing later debates about benchmark-vs-eval integrity when train and test share an environment.

Themes

  • 7 RL environments — uses MiniWoB / World of Bits as a sparse-reward RL environment for web tasks; central contribution is an exploration method for that environment.
  • 2 eval⇄capability⇄RL-env — the web-task suite is simultaneously the training signal and the evaluation benchmark; advancing capability and defining the eval are the same artifact.
  • 9 agent-specific — purpose-built for browser/web agents: DOM-tree observations, click/type action primitives, multi-step UI workflows.
  • 3 model/harness/skill (secondary) — introduces DOMNET, a harness/policy architecture tailored to the structured web observation space, plus the workflow-induction machinery.

Talks, podcasts & slides — transcribed + noted

47 of 48 transcribed (408k words of transcript) and deep-noted with verbatim [mm:ss] quotes. Pulled via youtube-transcript-api; notes in research/notes/talk-*.md.

  • How to look at your data — Jeff Huber & Jason Liu · AI Engineer World's Fair 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-huber-liu-look-at-your-data.md — A two-part talk: Huber (Chroma) on evaluating retrieval inputs, Liu on analyzing system outputs. The core thesis is "you can only manage what you measure,"
  • Failure is a Funnel — Bryan Bischof · Data Council 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-bischof-failure-is-a-funnel.md — Bischof argues that a single end-to-end "did the agent do the job?" pass/fail eval is necessary but radically insufficient for agentic systems, because capabili
  • Using LLMs as Judges: Insights, Challenges, Best Practices — Eugene Yan · Jason Liu series 2024 — ▶ video · note: talk-yan-llms-as-judges.md — A loose, three-way working session (Eugene Yan with Hamel Husain and Jason Liu) unpacking how to actually build LLM-as-a-judge evaluators in production rather t
  • Scaling Up Vibe Checks for LLMs — Shreya Shankar · Stanford MLSys #97 — ▶ video · note: talk-shankar-scaling-vibe-checks.md — Shankar (then a 3rd-year Berkeley PhD, databases + HCI) argues that the only task-specific eval most LLM-pipeline builders actually use — manually eyeballing ou
  • Why LLM Data Processing Pipelines Fail — Shreya Shankar · LangChain Interrupt 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-shankar-why-pipelines-fail.md — Shankar (UC Berkeley PhD, EVAPORATE/DocETL line of work) reframes the common "my prompts don't work, so just iterate on the prompts" advice as a misdiagnosis. F
  • Evals Are Not Unit Tests — Ido Pesok (Vercel v0) · AI Engineer 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-pesok-evals-not-unit-tests.md — An introductory, application-layer (not research-lab) framing of evals, built entirely around one extended analogy: an eval is a basketball court, not a unit-te
  • Building Metrics that actually work (workshop) — David Karam (Pi Labs) · AI Engineer 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-karam-metrics-that-work.md — This workshop, run by David Karam and a co-presenter (both ex-Google, over a decade building Search), argues that the hard part of LLM/agent evals is not "do I
  • From Self-driving to Autonomous Voice Agents — Brooke Hopkins (Coval) · AI Engineer 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-hopkins-self-driving-voice-agents.md — Hopkins, who led eval/simulation infrastructure at Waymo and now founded Coval (evals for voice agents), argues that the techniques that made self-driving cars
  • Fuzzing in the GenAI Era — Leonard Tang (Haize Labs) · AI Engineer 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-tang-fuzzing-genai.md — Tang argues that the standard eval recipe — a finite static golden dataset of inputs and ground-truth outputs, scored by some similarity metric — is fundamental
  • On Engineering AI Systems that Endure the Bitter Lesson — Omar Khattab (DSPy) · AI Engineer 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-khattab-systems-that-endure.md — Khattab argues that the AI-engineering treadmill — a new LLM, inference trick, or RL algorithm every week — is a symptom of building systems at the wrong level
  • Strategies for LLM Evals (harnesses workshop) — Taylor Jordan Smith · AI Engineer 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-smith-strategies-for-llm-evals.md — This is a hands-on workshop (not a polished keynote) from a Red Hat AI developer advocate that frames evals as the discipline enterprises need to safely move LL
  • The Future of Evals — Ankur Goyal (Braintrust) · AI Engineer 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-goyal-future-of-evals.md — This is a short (~5 minute) product-launch talk in which Goyal argues that the practice of running evals, while now critical to building the best AI products,
  • 3 Key Ideas in AI in 2025 (Verifier's Law) — Jason Wei (OpenAI) · Stanford AI Club 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-wei-3-key-ideas-2025.md — Wei lays out three frames for understanding AI in 2025, the middle of which — Verifier's Law — is the load-bearing idea for anyone building evals. The claim
  • Some Intuitions About Large Language Models — Jason Wei · The AI Conference 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-wei-intuitions-about-llms.md — Wei offers three mental models for why LLMs work: (1) next-word prediction at internet scale is massively multitask learning — a single objective implicitly t
  • Deep Dive into LLMs like ChatGPT — Andrej Karpathy · 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-karpathy-deep-dive-llms.md — Karpathy walks through the full LLM training pipeline (pre-training → supervised fine-tuning → reinforcement learning) and, in doing so, lays out the core disti
  • RLHF: Progress and Challenges — John Schulman · UC Berkeley EECS 2023 — ▶ video · note: talk-schulman-rlhf-progress-challenges.md — Schulman (co-founder of OpenAI, ChatGPT chief architect, PPO/TRPO author) argues that hallucination is fundamentally a training-objective problem, not a capac
  • Aligning Open Language Models — Nathan Lambert (Ai2) · Stanford CS25 V4 — ▶ video · note: talk-lambert-aligning-open-lms.md — Lambert narrates the ~14-month history of open post-training (alpaca → Vicuna → Zephyr → Tulu 2) since ChatGPT, and a recurring sub-theme is that the open ecosy
  • Building LLM Applications for Production — Chip Huyen · MLOps LLMs in Prod 2023 — ▶ video · note: talk-huyen-llm-apps-production.md — Chip Huyen walks through a ranked list of open, unsolved challenges in shipping LLM applications to production (circa mid-2023), explicitly reframing the talk a
  • The Model is the Product — Han-Chung Lee · Data Council 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-lee-model-is-the-product.md — Lee's thesis is that the foundation model — not the application wrapper around it — is where value concentrates, by analogy to how Intel's CPU dominated the PC
  • RL Environments at Scale — Will Brown (Prime Intellect) · AI Engineer 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-brown-rl-environments-at-scale.md — Brown argues that an RL environment, an eval, a synthetic-data engine, and a deployed agent are all the same object: a harness plus a collection of tasks plus
  • LLM benchmarks in the time of agents — Florian Brand (Prime Intellect) · Big Techday 26 (2026) — ▶ video · note: talk-brand-benchmarks-time-of-agents.md — Brand traces the history of LLM evaluation from static pre-training-knowledge quizzes (MMLU, TruthfulQA) through reasoning benchmarks (GPQA, HLE, FrontierMath)

  • Evals are the new PRD for AI products — Ankur Goyal (How I AI) · How I AI — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-howiai-goyal-evals-new-prd.md — Goyal (CEO of Braintrust) argues that evals are the modern PRD: a spec written largely in prose plus quantified examples that states what success looks like a
  • Ep 60: 10 Things I Hate About AI Evals — Hamel Husain (Vanishing Gradients) · Vanishing Gradients — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-vg60-10-things-i-hate.md — Hamel Husain reframes "evals" as fundamentally a data analysis / data science discipline applied to stochastic AI systems, not a software-test discipline — hi
  • Ep 50: A Field Guide to Rapidly Improving AI Products — Hamel Husain (Vanishing Gradients) · Vanishing Gradients — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-vg50-field-guide.md — Hamel Husain argues that "evals" is not a metric but an entire process: the disciplined work of looking at your data, doing error analysis, and externalizing wh
  • Five Hard-Earned Lessons About Evals — Ankur Goyal (Latent Space) · Latent Space — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-ls-goyal-five-lessons.md — Ankur Goyal (founder of Braintrust) distills five practical lessons from running evals across many production AI teams. His central argument is that good evals
  • Artificial Analysis: Independent LLM Evals — Cameron & Hill-Smith (Latent Space) · Latent Space — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-ls-artificial-analysis.md — Micah Hill-Smith and George Cameron, founders of Artificial Analysis, walk through how they run independent LLM evals at scale and why running them yourself — r
  • Reality: The Final Eval (Vending-Bench) — Petersson & Backlund (Andon Labs) · Latent Space / Cognitive Revolution — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-andon-reality-final-eval.md — Lucas Petersson and Axel Backlund of Andon Labs walk through Vending-Bench — a benchmark for long-term coherence of autonomous agents that has them run a simu
  • #5 Designing Evals — Vaibhav Gupta & Dex (AI That Works) · AI That Works — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-aitw5-designing-evals.md — Vaibhav (BAML/BoundaryML, ex-HoloLens) and Dex (HumanLayer) argue that evals are not a magic button you buy or build over six months, but an incremental enginee
  • #16 Evaluating Prompts Across Models — AI That Works · AI That Works — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-aitw16-compare-models.md — Vaibhav (Vibhav/"Vibbob," BAML) and Dex (HumanLayer, 12-Factor Agents) tackle a recurring practical question: when a new model drops, how do you decide whether
  • #24 Evals for Classification — AI That Works · AI That Works — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-aitw24-classification-evals.md — A live, screen-shared walkthrough of a production-grade evaluation harness for large-scale classification: routing a user query into one of ~1,400 hardware-stor
  • #34 Multimodal Evals — AI That Works · AI That Works — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-aitw34-multimodal-evals.md — Hosts Dexter and Vaibhav bring back Kevin Gregory (ML engineer at Evolution IQ, building insurance-claims guidance software) to walk through a real multimodal e
  • #372 It's 2026 and We're Still Talking Evals — Maggie Konstanty (MLOps Community) · MLOps Community — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-mlops372-still-talking-evals.md — Konstanty argues that evals are not a post-ship checklist item but a continuous practice that should start "the moment the idea of the product starts" and never
  • #728 Generative Benchmarking — Kelly Hong (TWIML) · TWIML AI — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-twiml728-generative-benchmarking.md — Kelly Hong (researcher at Chroma) argues that public retrieval benchmarks like MTEB are a poor basis for picking embedding models because they are generic, use
  • Shaping AI Benchmarks (HELM) — Percy Liang (Gradient Dissent) · Gradient Dissent — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-gd-liang-helm.md — Percy Liang (Stanford, co-founder of Together AI, creator of HELM) walks through why language models forced a rethink of benchmarking: a text-in/text-out object
  • Evaluating LLMs with Chatbot Arena — Joseph Gonzalez (Gradient Dissent) · Gradient Dissent — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-gd-gonzalez-chatbot-arena.md — Gonzalez (Berkeley; founder of Chatbot Arena / LM Arena and Run LLM) argues that correctness is only a small slice of what an eval should capture: human prefere
  • Evaluating AI, Designing for Non-Determinism — Aman Khan (Learning from ML) · Learning from Machine Learning — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-lfml-aman-khan.md — Aman Khan (head of product at Arize AI; formerly a self-driving test engineer at Cruise and an ML-platform PM at Spotify) argues that evaluating AI is not a fin
  • Karpathy: RL is terrible, why benchmarks mislead — Andrej Karpathy (Dwarkesh) · Dwarkesh Podcast — ▶ video · note: talk-pod-dwarkesh-karpathy.md — This is a commentator's synthesis of Karpathy's Dwarkesh appearance, organized around two theses: (1) we're in an "AGI bubble" of over-promised general intellig
  • How to Build AI Evals in 2026 (Step-by-Step) — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar · Aakash Gupta 2026 — ▶ video · note: talk-talk-hamel-shreya-build-evals-2026.md — Hamel and Shreya run a live, screen-shared walkthrough of building application-specific evals on real (anonymized) production data from Nurture Boss, an AI leas

  • Towards Building Safe and Secure Agentic AI — Dawn Song · Berkeley Advanced LLM Agents Sp25 — ▶ video · note: talk-song-safe-secure-agentic.md — Song frames the agent as a "hybrid/compound system" — symbolic components (OS, browser, functions) fused with non-symbolic neural components (LLMs) — and argues

  • Open-Source and Science in the Era of Foundation Models — Percy Liang · Berkeley LLM Agents MOOC F24 — ▶ video · note: talk-liang-opensource-science-fms.md — Liang's thesis is that "access shapes research" — the level at which you can touch a model (black-box API, open weights, or full open source) determines which q
  • CS336 Lecture 12: Evaluation — Hashimoto & Liang · Stanford CS336 2025 — ▶ video · note: talk-cs336-lec12-evaluation.md — A whirlwind survey of LLM evaluation, framed around one thesis: "there is no one true evaluation — it depends on the question you're trying to answer" (purchase

🖼 Slide decks (2)

  • LLM benchmarks in the era of agents (deck) — Florian Brand · TNG / Big Techday — [deck]((local slide deck)) · note: talk-brand-tng-deck.md

Library section audit (2026-06-24)

Per-section completeness verdicts + what was added. Run: 10 parallel scouts, URL-verified.

§1 — verdict

Solid on the 2024-early-2025 practitioner classics (Hamel, Shreya, Eugene Yan, Lambert, applied-llms), but missing the 2026 canonical updates that everyone now cites (Anthropic's Demystifying evals, Hamel/Shreya's Evals FAQ) plus the frontier-lab and contrarian/nuance angles. Biggest gap: nothing from 2026 and nothing from the labs themselves (Anthropic/OpenAI) on why evals matter.

Added (7): Evals Are NOT All You Need; Why AI evals are the hottest new skill for product builders; How evals drive the next chapter in AI for businesses; Beyond vibe checks: A PM's complete guide to evals; A pragmatic guide to LLM evals for devs; Predicting model behavior before release by simulating deployment (Deployment Simulation); evals are surprisingly often all you need

§2 — verdict

Solid on the thesis/blog side (Wei, Lee, Lambert, verifiers) but missing the empirical spine: the canonical RLVR papers (DeepSeek-R1, Tülu 3) that PROVE "verify it = train it," the reward-hacking-under-pressure evidence (Anthropic Nov-2025), the live environment marketplaces (Prime Intellect Hub, SemiAnalysis/Epoch market maps), and the Mechanize "environment quality is the bottleneck" essays. Adding these turns the section from claims into receipts.

Added (10): DeepSeek-R1: Incentivizing Reasoning Capability in LLMs via Reinforcement Learning; Tülu 3: Pushing Frontiers in Open Language Model Post-Training; Natural Emergent Misalignment from Reward Hacking in Production RL; Environments Hub: A Community Hub To Scale RL To Open AGI; How to fully automate software engineering; Cheap RL tasks will waste compute; An FAQ on Reinforcement Learning Environments; RL Environments and RL for Science: Data Foundries and Multi-Agent Architectures; Terminal-Bench: Benchmarking Agents on Hard, Realistic Tasks in Command Line Interfaces; tau2-bench (τ²-Bench): A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains

Remaining gaps: Could not confirm a stable open-access Nature URL for DeepSeek-R1 (s41586-025-09422-z redirects to auth); used the canonical arXiv abstract instead.; Did not find a single best 'evals define what you build / evals are products' practitioner essay at the Eugene-Yan bar specific to this RL-env framing beyond what's already listed (Wei/Lee cover it); may be worth a dedicated search if the curator wants the eval-design (not RL) angle.; OpenAI/Greg Brockman or Noam Brown talks on verifiable-reward RL were not deep-checked for a canonical citable artifact.

§3 — verdict

The section has the right core thesis (harness > model) and the Lee/Brand must-reads, but it is missing the canonical primary sources that DEFINE the decomposition — the Hamel-vs-Lee "Model is/not the Product" debate pair, Simon Willison's "agents = models using tools in a loop" definition, OpenAI's "Harness Engineering" Codex post, and the Anthropic primary docs for the "skill" leg (Agent Skills, writing tools, context engineering). The biggest gap is that the "skill" in the section title is currently unrepresented by any source.

Added (11): The Model is the Product; The Model is Not the Product; Agents are models using tools in a loop; Harness engineering: leveraging Codex in an agent-first world; Equipping agents for the real world with Agent Skills; Effective context engineering for AI agents; Writing effective tools for agents — with agents; Same Model, Different Results: Why Coding Agents Aren't Interchangeable; Holistic Agent Leaderboard (HAL); Agent Harness Engineering; What comes next with open models (weights / tools / harness decomposition)

§4 — verdict

Solid on concepts (five surfaces, three pillars, trajectory evals) and on schema (OpenInference), but missing the upstream OTel GenAI standard that OpenInference maps to, and missing the actual open-source observability+eval tooling (Langfuse, Phoenix, Weave, Laminar) plus ready-made trajectory evaluators (agentevals). Biggest gap: the section names schemas and vendors but lacks the canonical OTel GenAI spec and the open-source platforms practitioners actually instrument with.

Added (5): OpenTelemetry GenAI Semantic Conventions (agent & framework spans); Semantic Conventions for GenAI agent and framework spans; Inside the LLM Call: GenAI Observability with OpenTelemetry; W&B Weave — tracing & evaluation toolkit; Laminar — open-source observability for AI agents

Remaining gaps: No clearly canonical practitioner essay specifically on online vs offline evals / production monitoring as distinct grading surfaces — current results are mostly vendor guides (Braintrust, Galileo); a Eugene-Yan/Shreya-Shankar-tier primary source would strengthen this.; Memory and environment surfaces (named in the Lee five-surfaces piece) lack dedicated entries here, but the strongest sources for those (LoCoMo, LongMemEval) are benchmarks that likely belong in a benchmarks section rather than observability.; OpenLLMetry/Traceloop (vendor-neutral OTel instrumentation SDK) is a plausible add but overlaps heavily with the OTel GenAI spec and Langfuse/Phoenix entries, so omitted to avoid redundancy.

§5 — verdict

The eval-frameworks/harness coverage (5a-5d) is genuinely strong, but the section's own title promises "tracing, online/offline, CI" and that half is almost entirely missing: there are no tracing+eval observability platforms (Langfuse, Phoenix, Braintrust, Weave, LangSmith, Opik), no online/production-eval or guardrail-eval layer, no OTel GenAI standard, and none of the canonical statistical-rigor or agent-eval-process references a top curator would anchor on.

Added (3): Braintrust; RagaAI Catalyst; OpenAI Cookbook — Evals

Remaining gaps: Did not separately verify the exact live URL for the OTel GenAI semconv docs path (opentelemetry.io/docs/specs/semconv/gen-ai/) vs the blog index — confirm before listing.; LangSmith (LangChain's hosted tracing+eval platform) is a clear omission given agentevals/openevals are already listed, but I prioritized OSS-first picks; a curator may still want it explicitly.; Did not deep-dive CI-runner patterns specifically (e.g., promptfoo/Tonic-Validate GitHub Actions are listed, but a dedicated 'evals-in-CI' how-to reference like a GitHub Actions matrix-eval guide is still thin).; Synthetic dataset / eval-set generation as its own sub-area (beyond Ragas testset gen) is under-covered — e.g., DataDreamer or distilabel could merit a look.

§6 — verdict

Section 6 is strong on label-error case studies (HLE, FrontierMath, SWE-bench) and leaderboard gaming (Leaderboard Illusion), but it is missing the canonical contamination/overfitting measurement papers (GSM1k, Pervasive Label Errors, LiveBench/LiveCodeBench dynamic refresh), the statistical-rigor entry (Anthropic Error Bars), and a practitioner reference (HF Evaluation Guidebook). Biggest gap: no dynamic/contamination-resistant benchmark exemplar and no canonical "label errors destabilize benchmarks" foundation.

Added (8): A Careful Examination of LLM Performance on Grade School Arithmetic (GSM1k); Pervasive Label Errors in Test Sets Destabilize Machine Learning Benchmarks; Are We Done with MMLU? (MMLU-Redux); LiveCodeBench: Holistic and Contamination-Free Evaluation of LLMs for Code; LiveBench: A Challenging, Contamination-Limited LLM Benchmark; The LLM Evaluation Guidebook (Open LLM Leaderboard team); Holistic Agent Leaderboard: The Missing Infrastructure for AI Agent Evaluation; Gaming the System: Goodhart's Law Exemplified in the AI Leaderboard Controversy

Remaining gaps: No single dedicated contamination-detection survey is listed (candidates: arXiv 2502.14425 'A Survey on Data Contamination for LLMs', or 2404.00699 'Comprehensive Survey of Contamination Detection Methods') — a survey could anchor the detection-methods angle but is lower-priority than the measurement papers above.; FutureHouse 'Fantastic Bugs and Where to Find Them in AI Benchmarks' (arXiv 2511.16842) appeared in search as a possible recent label-error taxonomy entry but was not URL-verified in this pass.; Epoch AI's response to saturation (Epoch Capabilities Index / IRT-based aggregation) is a relevant 'how to measure past saturation' angle but I did not find a single canonical citable URL to verify — worth a follow-up search.

§7 — verdict

Section 7 as listed is thin (4 items) and leans heavily on Lambert blogs plus two reward-model/RLVR papers; it under-covers the canonical reward-hacking/specification-gaming lineage, practitioner voices on building environments (Will Brown), the Prime Intellect Environments Hub launch thesis, and difficulty-calibration/curriculum primary sources. Biggest gap: no canonical reward-hacking reference and no recent (Nov-2025) Anthropic emergent-misalignment-from-reward-hacking paper, both load-bearing for the reward-design portion of this section.

Added (8): Reward Hacking in Reinforcement Learning; Specification gaming: the flip side of AI ingenuity; Multi-Turn RL for Multi-Hour Agents — with Will Brown (Prime Intellect); Position: The Hidden Costs and Measurement Gaps of RLVR; RewardBench 2: Advancing Reward Model Evaluation; Reward Modeling (RLHF Book, ch. 5); Curriculum RL from Easy to Hard Tasks Improves LLM Reasoning (E2H Reasoner); GenEnv: Difficulty-Aligned Co-Evolution Between LLM Agents and Environment Simulators

§8 — verdict

Strong on practitioner alignment workflow (Yan/Hamel/Shankar) and the original MT-Bench bias numbers, but missing the core academic primary sources on bias (self-preference, G-Eval, the LLM-as-judge survey) and—critically for this section's own "verifiable vs judgeable" framing—the entire 2025 verifier literature (RewardBench 2, One-Token-to-Fool, Weaver, Agent-as-a-Judge, the RLVR-vs-judge framing).

Added (9): LLM Evaluators Recognize and Favor Their Own Generations; G-Eval: NLG Evaluation using GPT-4 with Better Human Alignment; A Survey on LLM-as-a-Judge; One Token to Fool LLM-as-a-Judge; Weaver: Closing the Generation-Verification Gap with Weak Verifiers; Agent-as-a-Judge: Evaluate Agents with Agents; VerifyBench: A Systematic Benchmark for Evaluating Reasoning Verifiers Across Domains; Enhancing LLM-as-a-Judge with Grading Notes / From Pilot to Production with Custom Judges; Justice or Prejudice? Quantifying Biases in LLM-as-a-Judge (CALM framework)

§9 — verdict

Strong on outcome-grading and pass@k, but missing the practitioner-canonical agent benchmarks already named-but-unverified in the brief (WebArena/OSWorld/Terminal-Bench/Cybench now have confirmed URLs), and has a notable gap on trajectory/LLM-judge evaluation, multi-agent failure analysis, and code "localization" (the section title promises these but lists nothing). Below fills those gaps with verified canonical + 2025-2026 sources.

Added (10): WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents; OSWorld: Benchmarking Multimodal Agents for Open-Ended Tasks in Real Computer Environments; Terminal-Bench: Benchmarking Agents on Hard, Realistic Tasks in Command-Line Interfaces; Cybench: A Framework for Evaluating Cybersecurity Capabilities and Risk of Language Models; AgentRewardBench: Evaluating Automatic Evaluations of Web Agent Trajectories; Why Do Multi-Agent LLM Systems Fail? (MAST taxonomy); AppWorld: A Controllable World of Apps and People for Benchmarking Interactive Coding Agents; BrowseComp: A Simple Yet Challenging Benchmark for Browsing Agents; LocAgent: Graph-Guided LLM Agents for Code Localization; WebVoyager: Building an End-to-End Web Agent with Large Multimodal Models

Remaining gaps: Localization-as-internationalization (multilingual/cultural agent eval, e.g. BrowseComp-ZH) exists but is niche; left out in favor of code-localization which better matches the section's other items — flag if i18n was the intended meaning.; No dedicated open-source agent-eval harness/observability tool listed (e.g. LangSmith/Braintrust/Galileo agentic metrics, OpenAI simple-evals) — the section is benchmark/paper-heavy and could use one tooling entry, but most are vendor-marketing-adjacent so curate carefully.; tau2-bench and SWE-bench are covered; no general long-horizon 'AI Agents That Matter' (Kapoor/Narayanan, arxiv 2407.01502) cost-vs-accuracy critique — strong candidate if a methodology-critique slot is wanted.

§10 — verdict

The section has good coverage on benchmark auditing (BenchJack), Dawn Song's work, and statistical rigor, but is missing nearly all of the field's canonical agent-security benchmarks (AgentDojo, AgentHarm, InjecAgent, Agent Security Bench), the foundational defense-by-design paper (CaMeL), Anthropic's own sabotage/insider-threat evals (SHADE-Arena, Agentic Misalignment), the standard practitioner red-teaming tooling (PyRIT), and the industry-standard threat taxonomies (OWASP Agentic Top 10, MITRE ATLAS). The biggest gap is the complete absence of the prompt-injection benchmark canon and Simon Willison's foundational conceptual framing.

Added (12): AgentDojo: A Dynamic Environment to Evaluate Prompt Injection Attacks and Defenses for LLM Agents; AgentHarm: A Benchmark for Measuring Harmfulness of LLM Agents; InjecAgent: Benchmarking Indirect Prompt Injections in Tool-Integrated LLM Agents; Defeating Prompt Injections by Design (CaMeL); The lethal trifecta for AI agents: private data, untrusted content, and external communication; SHADE-Arena: Evaluating Sabotage and Monitoring in LLM Agents; Agentic Misalignment: How LLMs Could Be Insider Threats; PyRIT — Python Risk Identification Tool for generative AI; OWASP Top 10 for Agentic Applications (2026) + LLM Applications (2025); MITRE ATLAS — Adversarial Threat Landscape for AI Systems; Agent Security Bench (ASB): Formalizing and Benchmarking Attacks and Defenses in LLM-based Agents; Gray Swan x UK AISI Agent Red-Teaming Challenge

Practitioner link graph (industry-side)

Link-recursion crawl over industry eval blogs (the blog-world analog of citations). 5,430 URL nodes, 29,100 link edges, depth 3. Ranked by cross-domain in-degree (distinct external sites linking in) to remove site-internal nav.

Known practitioner sources, ranked by cross-domain in-degree

Industry / practitioner discoveries (rebalancing pass)

Discovered 53 new INDUSTRY candidates; wrote 30 deep notes (arXiv excluded).

  • No Evals Dataset? Here's How to Build One from Scratch (AI Evals & Observability series) — Paul Iusztin (newsletter) — https://www.decodingai.com/p/build-an-ai-evals-dataset-with-error-analysis
  • What 1,200 Production Deployments Reveal About LLMOps in 2025 (+ the LLMOps Database) — Alex Strick van Linschoten (ZenML) (eng-blog) — https://www.zenml.io/blog/what-1200-production-deployments-reveal-about-llmops-in-2025
  • Vanishing Gradients (agents & evals episodes) — Hugo Bowne-Anderson — Hugo Bowne-Anderson (host); guests incl. Hamel Husain, Shreya Shankar, Ravin Kumar, Alex Strick van Linschoten (talk) — https://vanishinggradients.fireside.fm/
  • AI That Works — Eval Episodes (#5, #16, #24, #34) — Vaibhav Gupta (BoundaryML/BAML) & Dex Horthy (talk) — https://boundaryml.com/podcast
  • Calibrating Scores of LLM-as-a-Judge — GoDaddy Engineering (eng-blog) — https://www.godaddy.com/resources/news/calibrating-scores-of-llm-as-a-judge
  • Eval-driven development: Build better AI faster — Malte Ubl, Alice Alexandra Moore, Ido Pesok (Vercel) (eng-blog) — https://vercel.com/blog/eval-driven-development-build-better-ai-faster
  • AI Agent Evaluation: 5 Lessons Learned The Hard Way — Alik Peltinovich, Michael Segner, Elor Arieli, Lior Gavish (Monte Carlo) (eng-blog) — https://www.montecarlodata.com/blog-ai-agent-evaluation/
  • Evaluating AI agents: Real-world lessons from building agentic systems at Amazon — Yunfei Bai, Allie Colin, Kashif Imran, Winnie Xiong (AWS / Amazon) (eng-blog) — https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/machine-learning/evaluating-ai-agents-real-world-lessons-from-building-agentic-systems-at-amazon/
  • A pragmatic guide to LLM evals for devs — Gergely Orosz (Pragmatic Engineer) with Hamel Husain (newsletter) — https://newsletter.pragmaticengineer.com/p/evals
  • Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Runtime — Han-Chung Lee (blog) — https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/04/24/hidden-technical-debt-agent-runtime/
  • Evaluating AI Agents in Practice: Benchmarks, Frameworks, and Lessons Learned — Amit Kumar Padhy (eng-blog) — https://www.infoq.com/articles/evaluating-ai-agents-lessons-learned/
  • Macro Evals for Agentic Systems — Shikhar Kwatra, Will Thieme, Bradley Strauss (OpenAI Cookbook) (tool) — https://developers.openai.com/cookbook/examples/partners/macro_evals_for_agentic_systems/macro_evals_for_agentic_systems

  • AGENTS.md outperforms skills in our agent evals — Jude Gao (Vercel) (eng-blog) — https://vercel.com/blog/agents-md-outperforms-skills-in-our-agent-evals
  • Evaluating Deep Agents: Our Learnings — The LangChain Team (eng-blog) — https://www.langchain.com/blog/evaluating-deep-agents-our-learnings
  • Improve agent quality with Insights Agent and Multi-turn Evals, now in LangSmith — The LangChain Team (eng-blog) — https://www.langchain.com/blog/insights-agent-multiturn-evals-langsmith
  • Agent Evaluation: How to Evaluate LLM Agents — Langfuse (no individual byline) (eng-blog) — https://langfuse.com/guides/cookbook/example_pydantic_ai_mcp_agent_evaluation
  • Evaluate agent trajectories — Comet / Opik Documentation (tool) — https://www.comet.com/docs/opik/evaluation/evaluate_agent_trajectory

  • Agent Evaluation: A Detailed Guide — Cameron R. Wolfe (newsletter) — https://cameronrwolfe.substack.com/p/agent-evals

  • Introducing GBA Eval: Giving frontier coding agents 24 hours to write a Game Boy Advance emulator — Stephen Yang, Ege Erdil, Tamay Besiroglu (Mechanize) (eng-blog) — https://www.mechanize.work/technical-blog/introducing-gba-eval/

Eval canon by topic

Eval-relevant nodes grouped by topic, top 20 per topic by in-degree.

Benchmarks & integrity (392)

  • GLUE: A Multi-Task Benchmark and Analysis Platform for Natural Language Understanding (2018) — in-deg 54, cites 8654, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/1804.07461
  • SuperGLUE: A Stickier Benchmark for General-Purpose Language Understanding Systems (2019) — in-deg 45, cites 2813, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.00537
  • Natural Questions: A Benchmark for Question Answering Research (2019) — in-deg 44, cites 4804, depth 1
  • WebArena: A Realistic Web Environment for Building Autonomous Agents (2023) — in-deg 26, cites 1472, depth 0 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.13854
  • DROP: A Reading Comprehension Benchmark Requiring Discrete Reasoning Over Paragraphs (2019) — in-deg 21, cites 1310, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/1903.00161
  • SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues? (2023) — in-deg 18, cites 2599, depth 0 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770
  • Adversarial NLI: A New Benchmark for Natural Language Understanding (2019) — in-deg 16, cites 1229, depth 2 — https://arxiv.org/abs/1910.14599
  • CodeXGLUE: A Machine Learning Benchmark Dataset for Code Understanding and Generation (2021) — in-deg 14, cites 1492, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2102.04664
  • AGIEval: A Human-Centric Benchmark for Evaluating Foundation Models (2023) — in-deg 14, cites 847, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.06364
  • τ-bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains (2024) — in-deg 13, cites 682, depth 0 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.12045
  • GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants (2023) — in-deg 13, cites 885, depth 0 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.12983
  • Did Aristotle Use a Laptop? A Question Answering Benchmark with Implicit Reasoning Strategies (2021) — in-deg 13, cites 1073, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2101.02235
  • LiveCodeBench: Holistic and Contamination Free Evaluation of Large Language Models for Code (2024) — in-deg 12, cites 1691, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.07974
  • ALFRED: A Benchmark for Interpreting Grounded Instructions for Everyday Tasks (2019) — in-deg 12, cites 1109, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/1912.01734
  • Is Reinforcement Learning (Not) for Natural Language Processing?: Benchmarks, Baselines, and Building Blocks for Natural Language Policy Optimization (2022) — in-deg 11, cites 298, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2210.01241
  • PandaLM: An Automatic Evaluation Benchmark for LLM Instruction Tuning Optimization (2023) — in-deg 10, cites 386, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.05087
  • Stereotyping Norwegian Salmon: An Inventory of Pitfalls in Fairness Benchmark Datasets (2021) — in-deg 9, cites 378, depth 2
  • Dynabench: Rethinking Benchmarking in NLP (2021) — in-deg 8, cites 560, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2104.14337
  • API-Bank: A Benchmark for Tool-Augmented LLMs (2023) — in-deg 8, cites 104, depth 1
  • GPQA: A Graduate-Level Google-Proof Q&A Benchmark (2023) — in-deg 8, cites 2814, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.12022

LLM-as-judge & reward models (161)

Safety / adversarial (96)

  • RealToxicityPrompts: Evaluating Neural Toxic Degeneration in Language Models (2020) — in-deg 33, cites 1732, depth 1 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2009.11462

  108. Preference Ranking Optimization for Human Alignment (2023) — in-deg 7, cites 376 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.17492

  110. A Survey on Evaluation of Large Language Models (2023) — in-deg 7, cites 3457 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2307.03109
  111. The price of debiasing automatic metrics in natural language evalaution (2018) — in-deg 7, cites 135 — https://arxiv.org/abs/1807.02202
  112. The Secret Sharer: Evaluating and Testing Unintended Memorization in Neural Networks (2018) — in-deg 7, cites 1427 — https://arxiv.org/abs/1802.08232
  113. RAFT: Reward rAnked FineTuning for Generative Foundation Model Alignment (2023) — in-deg 6, cites 740 — https://arxiv.org/abs/2304.06767

Repos in the eval ecosystem

20 seed repos, with the arXiv papers they reference.

promptfoo/promptfoo · ⭐22516

Test your prompts, agents, and RAGs. Red teaming/pentesting/vulnerability scanning for AI. Compare performance of GPT, Claude, Gemini, DeepSeek, and more. Simple declarative configs with command line and CI/CD integration. Used by OpenAI and Anthropic. Home: https://promptfoo.dev

openai/evals · ⭐18756

Evals is a framework for evaluating LLMs and LLM systems, and an open-source registry of benchmarks.

EleutherAI/lm-evaluation-harness · ⭐13047

A framework for few-shot evaluation of language models. Home: https://www.eleuther.ai Papers: arXiv:2005.14165

SWE-bench/SWE-bench · ⭐5240

SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-world Github Issues? Home: https://www.swebench.com Papers: arXiv:2410.03859, arXiv:2310.06770, arXiv:2504.21798

PrimeIntellect-ai/verifiers · ⭐4227

Our library for RL environments + evals

anthropics/original_performance_takehome · ⭐3903

Anthropic's original performance take-home, now open for you to try!

openai/human-eval · ⭐3271

Code for the paper "Evaluating Large Language Models Trained on Code" Papers: arXiv:2107.03374

gkamradt/LLMTest_NeedleInAHaystack · ⭐2324

Doing simple retrieval from LLM models at various context lengths to measure accuracy

UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_ai · ⭐2242

Inspect: A framework for large language model evaluations Home: https://inspect.aisi.org.uk/

sierra-research/tau2-bench · ⭐1424

τ-Bench: A Benchmark for Tool-Agent-User Interaction in Real-World Domains Home: https://www.taubench.com Papers: arXiv:2506.07982, arXiv:2603.13686, arXiv:2603.04370, arXiv:2512.07850, arXiv:2406.12045

sierra-research/tau-bench · ⭐1293

Code and Data for Tau-Bench Papers: arXiv:2210.03629, arXiv:2406.12045, arXiv:2506.07982

carlini/yet-another-applied-llm-benchmark · ⭐1060

A benchmark to evaluate language models on questions I've previously asked them to solve.

Arize-ai/openinference · ⭐1049

OpenTelemetry Instrumentation for AI Observability Home: https://arize-ai.github.io/openinference/

braintrustdata/autoevals · ⭐943

AutoEvals is a tool for quickly and easily evaluating AI model outputs using best practices.

langchain-ai/agentevals · ⭐624

Readymade evaluators for agent trajectories

stanford-crfm/air-bench-2024 · ⭐30

AIR-Bench 2024 is a safety benchmark that aligns with emerging government regulations and company policies Papers: arXiv:2407.17436

irthomasthomas/undecidability · ⭐24

zohaib-khan5040/Countdown-Code · ⭐9

A testbed for studying the emergence and generalization of reward hacking Papers: arXiv:2603.07084

mohsenhariri/scorio.

Library provenance — timeline of what was added when

date pass what
2026-06-22→23 v1 build Initial library from Round-1 research + /deep-research synthesis: ~88 entries across the 10 themes + must-read starter set + companies.
2026-06-23 §5 tools sweep Expanded "Evaluation infrastructure" from ~11 to ~75 verified tools (7 sub-sections: frameworks, JS runners, RAG eval, judges/rewards, RL-environments, observability platforms, tracing standards).
2026-06-23 industry notes 30 new industry/practitioner deep notes added to research/notes (rebalance toward non-academic).
2026-06-24 BenchFlow + Harbor fix Added BenchFlow (benchflow-ai/benchflow) to §5e + companies; cross-listed Harbor into §5a; expanded the env-lab company list.
2026-06-24 section audit (✦) 10-scout per-section gap audit added 83 verified entries across all themes.
2026-06-24 talk-media pass Indexed + transcribed 47 talks/podcasts/lectures (408k words via youtube-transcript-api) + 2 slide decks; deep-noted each with verbatim [mm:ss] quotes; added the 🎙 library section.

Note on granularity: exact per-entry dating is reliable for the 2026-06-24 audit additions (snapshot taken pre-audit, 162 baseline URLs). Within the 06-23 build I did not snapshot before the §5 sweep, so §5's tools-vs-v1 split is noted at section level rather than per entry. Going forward, snapshots are taken at each pass for exact provenance.

Verified citations & source notes

From Round 1 mining + adversarial verification.

Eugene Yan (eugeneyan.com) — task-specific LLM evals, LLM-evaluator alignment, AlignEval, eval-driven development; plus the co-authored "What We Learned from a Year of Building with LLMs" (applied-llms.org)

Eugene Yan's body of work is the most practitioner-grounded playbook for productionizing LLM/agent evals. Across seven primary pieces he argues a consistent thesis: tooling and LLM-as-judge don't save a product — a disciplined process does. The durable contributions for an agent-evals book: (1) reframing evals as the scientific method / eval-driven development (TDD for AI), where an eval harness wired into the experiment pipeline is the real ROI because it tightens the iteration loop; (2) a strong bias toward binary classification (recall/precision/Cohen's kappa) over Likert and correlation metrics because binary labels align humans and judges better and translate to production; (3) the "look at the data first / work backward from data" discipline, grounded in criteria drift ("it is impossible to completely determine evaluation criteria prior to human judging of LLM outputs"); (4) a rigorous methodology for evaluating the evaluator — aligning LLM-judges to human labels on dev/test splits, benchmarking against human (not perfect) performance, controlling position/verbosity/self-enhancement bias, and choosing direct-scoring vs pairwise by objective-vs-subjective task; (5) concrete task recipes (classification distribution-separation diagnostics, NLI-finetuned consistency evals, translation metrics, copyright/toxicity) with the insight that off-the-shelf benchmarks rarely transfer; and (6) an agentic-eval template (sandboxed target, difficulty-controlling inputs, tools, deterministic grader) with outcome grading + partial-credit subtask ladders + transcript audits to catch reward hacking. Together these supply battle-tested principles, sharp distinctions (benchmark vs application eval, eval vs guardrail, eval vs agent environment, outcome vs method/trajectory, dev-evaluator vs prod-guardrail), and reusable frameworks (the eval flywheel, EDD, AlignEval's "Align AI to human, calibrate human to AI, repeat," product-evals-in-3-steps).

Reliability: solid — I fetched all 8 cited URLs (all real, all resolve, none invented). Every one of the 24 attributed quotes was found verbatim or near-verbatim on the page it is attributed to, and every article title + publication date in the attributions matches the live page (evals=Mar 2024, llm-evaluators=Aug 2024, aligneval=Oct 2024, eval-process=Apr 2025, product-evals=Nov 2025, cybersecurity-evals=June 2026; applied-llms.org authorship of all seven names including Eugene Yan confirmed). No misattributions found.

The highest-stakes non-quote claims were also confirmed against the pages: NLI finetuning ROC-AUC "from 0.56 (practically random) to 0.85"; Voiceflow catching a 10% drop on gpt-3.5-turbo-0301 -> -1106; the statistical-confidence sample sizing (200 samples -> 3% +/- 2.4%, 400 -> 3% +/- 1.7%, sqrt scaling); Cohen's kappa bands (0.2-0.3 human floor, 50% defects missed to fatigue, 0.4-0.6 substantial, 0.7 excellent); position bias (gpt-3.5 ~50%, claude-v1 ~70%) and verbosity bias (>90%); self-enhancement bias (gpt-4 +10%, claude-v1 +25%); criteria-drift quote from Shankar et al.'s EvalGen/"Who Validates the Validators"; PoLL ensemble at 1/7 cost; the 4-week harness war story; and the 68%->94% iterate-on-judge result on applied-llms.org. The Goodhart's Law and genchi-genbutsu quotes are correctly attributed as cited-within (Goodhart) and original framing.

Scope caveat: I verified the 24 listed quotes and the load-bearing numeric claims directly. I did NOT independently re-confirm every secondary item in the "examples" array (e.g., needle-in-haystack 80%/30% recall split, Harry Potter "first lines almost always work" copyright tip, Llama2 pairwise $3.5 vs $25 SFT economics, Judging-the-Judges kappa 0.84/0.79 vs human 0.97, the "~1000 samples" figure attached to the NLI result). These are consistent with the sourced pages and the surrounding verified claims, so they are plausible, but they sit one notch below the verbatim-verified quotes. Note also that cybersecurity-evals is dated June 2026 (the current month) — a very fresh source, which is fine but worth knowing it is brand new.

Overall this is an unusually clean, accurately-sourced findings set — the verbatim hit rate on quotes is 24/24 with correct attributions and real URLs.

Quotes

  • "If you've ran off-the-shelf evals for your tasks, you may have found that most don't work. They barely correlate with application-specific performance and aren't discriminative enough to use in production." — Eugene Yan, Task-Specific LLM Evals that Do & Don't Work (Mar 2024) (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/evals/)
  • "IMHO, accuracy is too coarse a metric to be useful." — Eugene Yan, Task-Specific LLM Evals that Do & Don't Work (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/evals/)
  • "Examining the separation of distributions is valuable because a model can have high ROC-AUC and PR-AUC but still not be suitable for production." — Eugene Yan, Task-Specific LLM Evals that Do & Don't Work (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/evals/)
  • "Don't be paralyzed by the need for perfection or zero risk, and as a result, succumb to Innovator's Dilemma. Instead, set realistic, risk-adjusted evaluation criteria, start small, collect feedback, and iterate frequently." — Eugene Yan, Task-Specific LLM Evals that Do & Don't Work (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/evals/)
  • "As a data point, the typical factual inconsistency/irrelevance rate is 5 - 10%, even after grounding via RAG and good prompt engineering. And from what I've learned from LLM providers, it may be prohibitively hard to go below 2%." — Eugene Yan, Task-Specific LLM Evals that Do & Don't Work (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/evals/)
  • "Building product evals is simply the scientific method in disguise. That's the secret sauce. It's a cycle of inquiry, experimentation, and analysis." — Eugene Yan, An LLM-as-Judge Won't Save The Product—Fixing Your Process Will (Apr 2025) (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/eval-process/)
  • "If teams don't apply the scientific method, practice eval-driven development, and monitor the system's output, buying or building yet another evaluation tool won't save the product." — Eugene Yan, An LLM-as-Judge Won't Save The Product—Fixing Your Process Will (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/eval-process/)
  • "Automated evaluators amplify our existing annotation and feedback processes." — Eugene Yan, An LLM-as-Judge Won't Save The Product—Fixing Your Process Will (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/eval-process/)
  • "Align AI to human. Calibrate human to AI. Repeat." — Eugene Yan, AlignEval: Building an App to Make Evals Easy, Fun, and Automated (Oct 2024) (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/aligneval/)
  • "Many teams make the mistake of crafting elaborate eval criteria without first looking at the data. It's like theorizing about user experience and defects from the ivory tower, without doing error analysis." — Eugene Yan, AlignEval (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/aligneval/)
  • "The way to solve this—and build useful evals—is to work backward from the data. That's what AlignEval tries to do by putting data and metrics first, not preset criteria or LLMs." — Eugene Yan, AlignEval (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/aligneval/)
  • "One anti-pattern is building a single "God Evaluator" ... that attempts to assess 5 - 10 dimensions—faithfulness, relevance, conciseness, tone, etc.—in one prompt. I've never seen this work well." — Eugene Yan, Product Evals in Three Simple Steps (Nov 2025) (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/product-evals/)
  • "The benchmark is human performance, not perfection." — Eugene Yan, Product Evals in Three Simple Steps (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/product-evals/)
  • "In my opinion, the true benefit isn't higher accuracy than human annotators—it's scalability. A well-aligned evaluator allows us to apply consistent, (super)human-level judgment across hundreds of samples in minutes, 24/7, without being bottlenecked by human review." — Eugene Yan, Product Evals in Three Simple Steps (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/product-evals/)
  • "If we want to assess a model migration from Claude Haiku 3.5 to Haiku 4.5, we make a one-line config change, start the pipeline, get lunch, and check results." — Eugene Yan, Product Evals in Three Simple Steps (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/product-evals/)
  • "I tend to be skeptical of correlation metrics. They don't account for chance agreement and thus could be overoptimistic (though Cohen's κ is an exception). ... Thus, where possible, I have my evaluators return binary outputs." — Eugene Yan, Evaluating the Effectiveness of LLM-Evaluators (aka LLM-as-Judge) (Aug 2024) (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/)
  • "If using it as a guardrail in production (low latency, high throughput), consider investing in finetuning a classifier or reward model, bootstrapping it on open-source data and labels you've collected during internal evals." — Eugene Yan, Evaluating the Effectiveness of LLM-Evaluators (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/)
  • "Gpt-4 favored itself with a 10% higher win rate while claude-v1 favored itself with a 25% higher win rate." — Eugene Yan, Evaluating the Effectiveness of LLM-Evaluators (summarizing Zheng et al., MT-Bench) (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/)
  • "The LLM-evaluators had high variance in correlation with human judgments across the datasets. Each model performed poorly on some datasets, suggesting that they're not reliable enough to systematically replace human judgments." — Eugene Yan, Evaluating the Effectiveness of LLM-Evaluators (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/)
  • "We like to use the following "intern test" when evaluating generations: If you took the exact input to the language model, including the context, and gave it to an average college student in the relevant major as a task, could they succeed? How long would it take?" — Yan, Bischof, Frye, Husain, Liu, Shankar — What We Learned from a Year of Building with LLMs (https://applied-llms.org/)
  • "Input-output pairs from production are the "real things, real places" (genchi genbutsu) of LLM applications, and they cannot be substituted." — Yan et al., What We Learned from a Year of Building with LLMs (https://applied-llms.org/)
  • "These "vibe checks" are signals of bad outputs; code and assertions operationalize them." — Yan et al., What We Learned from a Year of Building with LLMs (https://applied-llms.org/)
  • "LLM-as-Judge is not a silver bullet though. There are subtle aspects of language where even the strongest models fail to evaluate reliably. In addition, we've found that conventional classifiers and reward models can achieve higher accuracy than LLM-as-Judge, and with lower cost and latency." — Yan et al., What We Learned from a Year of Building with LLMs (https://applied-llms.org/)
  • "When a measure becomes a target, it ceases to be a good measure." — Goodhart's Law, cited in Yan et al., What We Learned from a Year of Building with LLMs (https://applied-llms.org/)
  • "Before diving into the benchmarks, I think it helps to understand the common pattern they share, largely based on four primitives. (You'll notice that it's similar to general evals and agent environments, albeit tweaked for the cybersecurity domain.)" — Eugene Yan, Patterns for Building Cybersecurity Evals (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/cybersecurity-evals/)
  • "Because exploitation is open-ended, most benchmarks evaluate outcomes instead of the method used." — Eugene Yan, Patterns for Building Cybersecurity Evals (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/cybersecurity-evals/)

Sources read: https://eugeneyan.com/writing/, https://eugeneyan.com/writing/evals/, https://eugeneyan.com/writing/eval-process/, https://eugeneyan.com/writing/aligneval/, https://eugeneyan.com/writing/product-evals/, https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/, https://eugeneyan.com/writing/cybersecurity-evals/, https://applied-llms.org/

Hidden gems: AlignEval (aligneval.com) — playable app + write-up (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/aligneval/); Patterns for Building Cybersecurity Evals (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/cybersecurity-evals/); Product Evals in Three Simple Steps (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/product-evals/); Who Validates the Validators (Shankar et al.) — 'criteria drift' (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/); Replacing Judges with Juries (PoLL) (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/)

Han-Chung Lee (@HanchungLee), "Han, Not Solo" blog — leehanchung.github.io. Director of ML at Moody's; curator of Deep Learning Weekly. Primary material: his "Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems" series (Agent Evaluation Infrastructure, Agent Harness), "A Taxonomy of RL Environments for LLM Agents," "LLM-as-a-Judge," "pass@k and Unbiased Estimator," and the "Statistics for AI/ML" series.

Han-Chung Lee's distinctive contribution to agent evals is treating evaluation as infrastructure and applied science rather than a metric library or a SaaS purchase. His through-line: chat eval was a spreadsheet (one prompt, one output, one score); agent eval is a system because agents complete tasks by mutating an environment, so the final output is only one of several artifacts you must grade. He formalizes the same object two ways — as an RL environment E={T,H,V,S,C} (tasks, harness, verifier, state, configuration) and as an eval harness — and argues a benchmark is just a frozen version of that environment. From this he derives concrete, non-obvious engineering requirements: capture trajectories/traces and per-step state deltas (not just outputs), run evals as controlled experiments (perturbation + ablation, paired runs with confidence intervals and minimum detectable effect), decouple the task from the harness so a model change and a harness change don't land in the same number, and build checkpoint/branch/replay so failures are reproducible and attributable. On LLM-as-judge he reframes skepticism as a judge-alignment problem (align judge to SMEs with rank-agreement stats, reasoning-before-score, perturbation tests for position bias, binary for verifiable tasks and rubric scales for ambiguous ones). He pairs this with statistical rigor (unbiased pass@k estimator, bootstrap CIs, Cohen's kappa for annotator agreement). The most quotable theme: "Agents do not only need datasets. Agents need worlds. Evaluation infrastructure is how those worlds become measurable."

Reliability: solid — Strong source integrity. All six leehanchung.github.io URLs cited for direct quotes resolve to real, live pages whose titles, authors, and publication dates match the attributions exactly (eval-infra 2026-06-13; RL-environments 2026-03-21; LLM-as-a-Judge 2024-08-11; pass@k 2025-09-08; agent-harness 2026-05-08; Cohen's-kappa 2025-03-03; plus first-principles-eval 2024-05-22 confirmed real). No invented URLs and no fabricated quotes found.

Of the 31 marquee quotes I checked against the live pages, 29 are verified verbatim or near-verbatim (distinctive lead clauses confirmed, with only trailing fragments unreturned by the fetch). Two are 'plausible' only because the exact full phrasing wasn't returned verbatim, though both are clearly consistent with the page: the closing 'Agents do not only need datasets...' line, and 'If your eval does not capture the state delta...'. Nothing reads as fabricated.

High-stakes specific claims also check out on the source pages: the five evaluation surfaces are named verbatim ('output, trace, memory, environment, and mechanistic interpretability'); the pass@k worked example (n=10, c=3 -> naive 83.2% / unbiased 91.7%, 1 - 21/252) is exact; the Letta Code 59.1% vs Claude Code 41.6% on Opus 4.5 figure and the 'harness is load-bearing' framing are present; LLM number-bias examples (GPT-42, gpt-3.5-turbo-skews-to-7) and Sclar et al 76-accuracy-point formatting result are present; the Eugene Yan review credit on the LLM-as-Judge post is confirmed.
