Only one genuine gap: the 'SMEs disagree in medicine' supporting detail could not be located in either statistics-series page it would most plausibly come from. It is a minor, non-quoted aside, so it does not move the overall assessment. Recommend either sourcing it precisely or softening it to the author's general claim. Bottom line: this source profile is safe to publish as-is, with the SME-in-medicine aside being the one line to double-check before print.

Quotes

  • "These scores are useful, but they are not the evaluation infrastructure. They are the artifacts left behind by a much larger system: task definitions, runtimes, snapshots, trace capture, state diffs, verifiers, judge calibration, replay, release gates, and production feedback." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "Scoring the final response is the easy part. The harder problem is building a data plane rich enough that the score is explainable, reproducible, and actionable." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "agents complete tasks by changing the environment, so final-output-only evals are no longer sufficient." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "Traces should not be scored primarily on whether they follow one golden path. Golden paths are useful for protocol compliance, regression tests, and regulated workflows, but they are a weak measure of agent intelligence." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "The canonical trace-level failure is the empty tool result hallucination." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "A polished final answer does not mean its faithful to the context. A tool call can be syntactically valid but semantically useless. A response can be high quality while the path that produced it is unsafe or excessive." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "A 2.4-point move in an average can be a real improvement or pure noise; Simpson's paradox applies, and the mean alone will not tell you which." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "The useful question is not "did the number go up"; it is "which component did we change that caused which tasks flipped and in which direction."" — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "And an eval infrastructure that cannot restore the state to a checkpoint is logs, not eval infra." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "State is the part of evaluation infrastructure that traditional ML teams tend to underestimate. They are used to datasets. Agents need worlds. Agentic evaluation infra needs multiverses." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "This is cargo cult evaluation - the number moved up and to the right because the measurement changed, not because the agent improved." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "Agents do not only need datasets. Agents need worlds. Evaluation infrastructure is how those worlds become measurable." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "If your eval does not capture the state delta, it is not flexible enough to evaluate tasks that are stateful." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "If memory is part of the harness, it is part of the evaluation surface." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "One bad episode becomes a durable preference. A polluted memory narrows exploration, repeats mistakes, and makes the agent look more confident as it gets worse." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "A benchmark is a frozen environment." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure' (2026-06-13) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/)
  • "A trajectory is what the trainer sees (state-action-reward tuples); a trace is what the observability system sees (structured execution logs)." — Han-Chung Lee, 'A Taxonomy of RL Environments for LLM Agents' (2026-03-21) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/03/21/rl-environments-for-llm-agents/)
  • "Verifiable beats judgeable. Programmatic checks such as string match or code execution, are faster, cheaper, and more consistent than LLM-as-judge. Use LLM-as-judge when there's no other option, not as the default." — Han-Chung Lee, 'A Taxonomy of RL Environments for LLM Agents' (2026-03-21) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/03/21/rl-environments-for-llm-agents/)
  • "Static rubrics get gamed. Models learn to write answers that score well on your rubric rather than solving the problem." — Han-Chung Lee, 'A Taxonomy of RL Environments for LLM Agents' (2026-03-21) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/03/21/rl-environments-for-llm-agents/)
  • "If you've built benchmarks before, you've already built an RL environment — just a frozen one." — Han-Chung Lee, 'A Taxonomy of RL Environments for LLM Agents' (2026-03-21) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/03/21/rl-environments-for-llm-agents/)
  • "This is the generation-verification gap: generating outputs with AI agents is cheap, but verifying their quality becomes progressively harder as tasks grow more open-ended." — Han-Chung Lee, 'A Taxonomy of RL Environments for LLM Agents' (2026-03-21) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/03/21/rl-environments-for-llm-agents/)
  • "Using the same model family to both generate completions and judge them creates a feedback loop — the agent learns to write prose that sounds good to its own judge rather than prose that's correct." — Han-Chung Lee, 'A Taxonomy of RL Environments for LLM Agents' (2026-03-21) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/03/21/rl-environments-for-llm-agents/)
  • "Get these right, and the agent learns behaviors that transfer to production. Get them wrong, and you've trained an expensive demo." — Han-Chung Lee, 'A Taxonomy of RL Environments for LLM Agents' (2026-03-21) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/03/21/rl-environments-for-llm-agents/)
  • "While some express skepticism about the quality and trustworthiness of LLMs as judges, the real question is how to ensure that model-based evaluations align with expectations." — Han-Chung Lee, 'LLM-as-a-Judge: Rethinking Model-Based Evaluations in Text Generation' (2024-08-11) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2024/08/11/llm-as-a-judge/)
  • "Avoid using [0.0,1.0], [1,10], and [1, 100] as LLMs may have inherent biases toward certain numbers." — Han-Chung Lee, 'LLM-as-a-Judge: Rethinking Model-Based Evaluations in Text Generation' (2024-08-11) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2024/08/11/llm-as-a-judge/)
  • "Note that LLMs generate answers autoregressively, so it's crucial to place reasoning_steps field before the score field." — Han-Chung Lee, 'LLM-as-a-Judge: Rethinking Model-Based Evaluations in Text Generation' (2024-08-11) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2024/08/11/llm-as-a-judge/)
  • "Just as we manage human judges, we should also manage our LLM-as-a-Judge." — Han-Chung Lee, 'LLM-as-a-Judge: Rethinking Model-Based Evaluations in Text Generation' (2024-08-11) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2024/08/11/llm-as-a-judge/)
  • "NOTE: this measure is intended to compare labeling by different annotators, not a classifier versus a ground truth." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Statistics for AI/ML, Part 3: Measuring Classification Agreements Between Agents' (2025-03-03) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2025/03/03/cohen-kappa/)
  • "The most under-discussed property of the harness is that the production harness and the training harness are not the same artifact, and increasingly should not be." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Harness' (2026-05-08) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/05/08/hidden-technical-debt-agent-harness/)
  • "Drop the same model into a third-party harness with different tool descriptions, a different loop shape, or a different memory layout and you are running it out-of-distribution: the model is being asked to act through an interface it was never optimized against." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Harness' (2026-05-08) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/05/08/hidden-technical-debt-agent-harness/)
  • "pass@1 is not about "passing on the first try" but rather the probability of a single sample being correct." — Han-Chung Lee, 'Statistics for AI/ML, Part 4: pass@k and Unbiased Estimator' (2025-09-08) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2025/09/08/pass-at-k/)

Sources read: https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/, https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/03/21/rl-environments-for-llm-agents/, https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2024/08/11/llm-as-a-judge/, https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2025/09/08/pass-at-k/, https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/05/08/hidden-technical-debt-agent-harness/, https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2025/03/03/cohen-kappa/, https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2024/05/22/first-principles-eval/, https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/, https://leehanchung.github.io/about/, https://leehanchung.github.io/talks/

Hidden gems: Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Evaluation Infrastructure (Lee, 2026) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/06/13/hidden-technical-debt-agent-evaluation-infra/); A Taxonomy of RL Environments for LLM Agents (Lee, 2026) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/03/21/rl-environments-for-llm-agents/); LLM-as-a-Judge: Rethinking Model-Based Evaluations (Lee, 2024) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2024/08/11/llm-as-a-judge/); Statistics for AI/ML, Part 4: pass@k and Unbiased Estimator (Lee, 2025) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2025/09/08/pass-at-k/); Hidden Technical Debt of AI Systems: Agent Harness (Lee, 2026) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2026/05/08/hidden-technical-debt-agent-harness/); Statistics for AI/ML, Part 3: Cohen's Kappa (Lee, 2025) (https://leehanchung.github.io/blogs/2025/03/03/cohen-kappa/); Ofir Press — How to Build Good Language Modeling Benchmarks (https://ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/); Ai2 olmo-eval (task/harness decoupling) (https://allenai.org/blog); Shankar et al — Who Validates the Validators? (EvalGen / criteria drift) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.12272)

Hamel Husain (hamel.dev) — primary posts: "Your AI Product Needs Evals" (/blog/posts/evals/), "Creating a LLM-as-a-Judge That Drives Business Results" (/blog/posts/llm-judge/), "Frequently Asked Questions (And Answers) About AI Evals" with Shreya Shankar (/blog/posts/evals-faq/), and "A Field Guide to Rapidly Improving AI Products" (/blog/posts/field-guide/)

Hamel Husain's body of work is the de-facto practitioner playbook for evaluating LLM/agent products. Its through-line: stop reaching for generic metrics and dashboards, and instead do error analysis — read your own traces, label failures, build a problem-specific taxonomy, and only then write evaluators for the failures you actually observed. Key contributions for an agent-evals book: (1) the eval flywheel (evaluate / debug / change-behavior) and the observation that most teams only do the third; (2) a rigorous, repeatable LLM-as-judge alignment process ("critique shadowing") that insists the human process — not the automated judge — is where value is created; (3) the binary-pass/fail-over-Likert argument; (4) the "benevolent dictator" single-domain-expert model and a ban on outsourcing error analysis; (5) concrete agent/multi-turn techniques — annotate the first upstream failure, use transition failure matrices, and validate judges with TPR/TNR on a held-out set rather than raw agreement; (6) a cost hierarchy of evaluators (cheap code assertions before expensive LLM judges before A/B tests) and roadmaps that count experiments, not features. War stories from Rechat/Lucy, Honeycomb, and Nurture Boss ground every claim.

Reliability: solid — Fact-check of the Hamel Husain (hamel.dev) findings. I fetched all four cited URLs and verified every quote and the highest-stakes claims against the live page text (with targeted second-pass checks on the most specific items to guard against over-eager summarizer confirmation).

ALL FOUR URLS ARE REAL AND RESOLVE, and each contains the content attributed to it:

  • https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals/ ("Your AI Product Needs Evals")
  • https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/ ("Creating a LLM-as-a-Judge That Drives Business Results")
  • https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/ (FAQ, w/ Shreya Shankar)
  • https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/field-guide/ ("A Field Guide to Rapidly Improving AI Products")

All 26 quotes in the quotes[] array were found verbatim (one has a trivial sentence-start capitalization difference — see checkedQuotes). Attributions to specific posts are correct in every case I checked.

KEY CLAIMS VERIFIED:

  • Three-level cost hierarchy: confirmed verbatim headings on /evals/ ("Level 1: Unit Tests", "Level 2: Human & Model Eval", "Level 3: A/B Testing").
  • Rechat UUID unit test + "Rechat has hundreds of these unit tests": confirmed on /evals/. Exact source line: "CRM results returned to the LLM contain fields that shouldn't be surfaced to the user; such as the UUID associated with an entry."
  • "benevolent dictator": correctly located in /evals-faq/ (NOT in /llm-judge/, which instead uses "Principal Domain Expert" — the findings get this attribution split right; no misattribution).
  • Honeycomb/Phillip "three iterations to achieve > 90% agreement": verbatim on /llm-judge/. Honeycomb is explicitly named ("When I was helping Honeycomb build their Query Assistant feature...").
  • TPR/TNR on a held-out set: principle #8 is grounded. The TPR/TNR-on-held-out-test-set language appears verbatim in /evals-faq/ ("Focus on achieving high True Positive Rate (TPR) and True Negative Rate (TNR) with your judge on a held out labeled test set."). The /llm-judge/ post expresses the same idea as "precision and recall separately" and the "raw agreement... misleading when classes are imbalanced" quote (verified there). So the concept is correctly sourced even though the cited llm-judge quote itself doesn't contain "TPR/TNR".
  • Nurture Boss: verbatim on /field-guide/ — spreadsheet (one row per conversation), open-ended notes, LLM-built taxonomy; date-handling success "improved from 33% to 95%".
  • Supporting in-principle phrasings also verified: "Generic metrics are worse than useless" (in /field-guide/, not /evals/) and "Teams with thoughtfully designed data viewers iterate 10x faster than those without them" (/field-guide/) — backs the "~10x faster" claim in principle #2.
  • "Outsourcing error analysis is usually a big mistake (with some exceptions)" confirmed verbatim in /evals-faq/.

No invented URLs, no misattributions, no fabricated quotes detected. The findings are unusually clean for fact-checking purposes. Only nitpicks are trivial typographic differences (capitalization / quote marks) noted below — none change meaning.

Quotes

  • "Many people focus exclusively on #3 above, which prevents them from improving their LLM products beyond a demo." — Hamel Husain, 'Your AI Product Needs Evals' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals/)
  • "You must remove all friction from the process of looking at data." — Hamel Husain, 'Your AI Product Needs Evals' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals/)
  • "Don't rely on generic evaluation frameworks to measure the quality of your AI. Instead, create an evaluation system specific to your problem." — Hamel Husain, 'Your AI Product Needs Evals' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals/)
  • "You can never stop looking at data—no free lunch exists." — Hamel Husain, 'Your AI Product Needs Evals' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals/)
  • "The important part is that these assertions should run fast and cheaply as you develop your application so that you can run them every time your code changes." — Hamel Husain, 'Your AI Product Needs Evals' (on Level 1 unit tests) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals/)
  • "I've often found that it's better to build my own data viewing & labeling tool so I can gather all the information I need onto one screen." — Hamel Husain, 'Your AI Product Needs Evals' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals/)
  • "You should track the correlation between model-based and human evaluation to decide how much you can rely on automatic evaluation." — Hamel Husain, 'Your AI Product Needs Evals' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals/)
  • "Tracking a bunch of scores on a 1-5 scale is often a sign of a bad eval process" — Hamel Husain, 'Creating a LLM-as-a-Judge That Drives Business Results' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/)
  • "What makes something a 3 versus a 4? Nobody knows, and different evaluators often interpret these scales differently." — Hamel Husain, 'Creating a LLM-as-a-Judge That Drives Business Results' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/)
  • "When domain experts must decide if an interaction passes or fails, they are compelled to articulate their expectations clearly." — Hamel Husain, 'Creating a LLM-as-a-Judge That Drives Business Results' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/)
  • "In most organizations there is usually one (maybe two) key individuals whose judgment is crucial for the success of your AI product." — Hamel Husain, 'Creating a LLM-as-a-Judge That Drives Business Results' (the Principal Domain Expert) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/)
  • "Most importantly, the critique should be detailed enough so that you can use it in a few-shot prompt for a LLM judge. In other words, it should be detailed enough that a new employee could understand it." — Hamel Husain, 'Creating a LLM-as-a-Judge That Drives Business Results' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/)
  • "However, using raw agreement is generally not recommended and can be misleading when classes are imbalanced." — Hamel Husain, 'Creating a LLM-as-a-Judge That Drives Business Results' (on judge alignment metrics) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/)
  • "It took us only three iterations to achieve > 90% agreement between the LLM and Phillip." — Hamel Husain, 'Creating a LLM-as-a-Judge That Drives Business Results' (Honeycomb case study) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/)
  • "Remember, the whole point of the LLM as a judge is to help you find these errors, so it's totally fine if you find them earlier!" — Hamel Husain, 'Creating a LLM-as-a-Judge That Drives Business Results' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/)
  • "Error analysis is the most important activity in evals." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'Frequently Asked Questions (And Answers) About AI Evals' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "Appointing a single domain expert as a 'benevolent dictator' is the most effective approach." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'Frequently Asked Questions (And Answers) About AI Evals' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "Write evaluators for errors you discover, not errors you imagine." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'Frequently Asked Questions (And Answers) About AI Evals' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "As a rule of thumb, you should aim to review at least 100 traces. My rough heuristic is if ~20 traces don't turn up a new category, you can stop (but review at least 100 to start)." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'Frequently Asked Questions (And Answers) About AI Evals' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "Annotate only the first failure in the trace initially—don't worry about downstream failures since these often cascade from the first issue." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'Frequently Asked Questions (And Answers) About AI Evals' (debugging multi-turn traces) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "Use transition failure matrices to understand error patterns. Create a matrix where rows represent the last successful state and columns represent where the first failure occurred." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'Frequently Asked Questions (And Answers) About AI Evals' (evaluating agentic workflows) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "Error analysis - the single most valuable activity in AI development and consistently the highest-ROI activity." — Hamel Husain, 'A Field Guide to Rapidly Improving AI Products' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/field-guide/)
  • "The single most impactful investment I've seen AI teams make isn't a fancy evaluation dashboard – it's building a customized interface that lets anyone examine what their AI is actually doing." — Hamel Husain, 'A Field Guide to Rapidly Improving AI Products' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/field-guide/)
  • "The people best positioned to improve your AI system are often the ones who know the least about AI." — Hamel Husain, 'A Field Guide to Rapidly Improving AI Products' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/field-guide/)
  • "The key metric for AI roadmaps isn't features shipped – it's experiments run." — Hamel Husain, 'A Field Guide to Rapidly Improving AI Products' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/field-guide/)
  • "Without this validation step, automated evaluations often drift from human expectations over time, especially as the distribution of inputs changes." — Hamel Husain, 'A Field Guide to Rapidly Improving AI Products' (maintaining trust in evals) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/field-guide/)

Sources read: https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals/, https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/, https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/, https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/field-guide/

Hidden gems: Frequently Asked Questions (And Answers) About AI Evals — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/); A Field Guide to Rapidly Improving AI Products — Hamel Husain (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/field-guide/); AI Evals course (Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)

Shreya Shankar (UC Berkeley) — research and writing on LLM/agent evaluation: "Who Validates the Validators?"/EvalGen (UIST 2024), SPADE (VLDB 2024), DocETL (VLDB 2025), the "AI Evals FAQ" co-authored with Hamel Husain, and her sh-reya.com blog ("In Defense of AI Evals," "Data Flywheels for LLM Applications").

Shankar's body of work supplies the empirical and conceptual backbone for modern agent/LLM evaluation. Her signature contribution is "criteria drift": the finding that you cannot fully specify evaluation criteria before looking at outputs, because grading outputs is what teaches you your criteria. This reframes evals from a static, a-priori benchmark into an iterative, data-grounded sensemaking loop. EvalGen operationalizes aligning LLM judges to human grades (coverage vs false-failure-rate tradeoff); SPADE mines prompt version history to synthesize assertions; the AI Evals FAQ codifies error analysis (open + axial coding to saturation), binary grading, multi-turn/agent trajectory evaluation (first-upstream-failure + transition failure matrices), and guardrails-vs-evaluators operational distinctions. Together they argue evals are not a vanity dashboard but the core development activity, and that you can't trust an automated judge you haven't validated against your own labels.

Reliability: mostly-solid — Overall this is an unusually clean, well-sourced findings set. I verified all 17 notable quotes verbatim against primary sources: I extracted the full text of 'Who Validates the Validators?' from the arXiv PDF (arXiv 2404.12272) and live-fetched the two sh-reya.com blog posts and the hamel.dev LLM Evals FAQ. Every WVV quote (cannot-be-trusted, catch-22, criteria-drift, P8 spectrum, P8 80%/escape-hatches, P9 maintain-evals) is verbatim; every blog quote (lying to themselves, error analysis is evaluation, systematic measurement of application quality, can't-theorize-criteria, examine-actual-data, GPT smell, binary metrics) is verbatim; every FAQ quote (most important activity, fixing upstream failures, ~20/100 traces, don't sell evals) is verbatim, re-confirmed on a skeptical second fetch quoting surrounding context (the FAQ's first fetch returned a blanket 'all EXACT', so I re-checked).

Attributions and metadata all check out: WVV author list (Shankar, Zamfirescu-Pereira, Hartmann, Parameswaran, Arawjo) and UIST 2024 venue correct; SPADE is VLDB 2024 (PVLDB vol 17, p4173) with title 'SPADE: Synthesizing Data Quality Assertions for Large Language Model Pipelines' (the arXiv v1 preprint used a slightly different title, 'Synthesizing Assertions...', but the finding uses the published title — correct); DocETL is VLDB 2025 (PVLDB vol 18, p3035); blog dates (Jul 1, 2024 and Sep 5, 2025) correct. All cited URLs are real and resolve — no invented URLs.

Quantitative claims I independently confirmed in the WVV text: coverage 49%->73% with an assertion set 'less than half the size of SPADE's' (verbatim); medical pipeline = 84 doctor-patient transcripts, 68% good; product pipeline = 100 Amazon products, 51% good; 9 industry-practitioner participants; NER-on-tweets task; thumbs-up implies passing ALL criteria (verbatim 'if a response is given a thumbs-up, it is assumed to pass all'); proper-noun drift (P2, P8) relaxing to 'most ... entities were proper [nouns]'; #justdoit/#Nike example with P5 (didn't want Nike extracted) vs P9 (wanted Nike but not the hashtag) and the shared hashtag-character assertion; the 'is alignment an actualizable goal? ... Is validating the validators only ever a work-in-progress?' epistemic close (verbatim); and the 'I know it when I see it' obscenity analogy (paper cites Gewirtz 1996 ref [17]; the finding's added detail 'Justice Potter Stewart / 1964 obscenity case' is correct background — Jacobellis v. Ohio — though that name/date is supplied by the writer, not stated in the paper). SPADE's 9-category prompt-delta taxonomy and '14% fewer assertions / 21% fewer false failures' are verbatim from the SPADE abstract; DocETL's 'rewrite directives', agent-guided plan evaluation, and '25-80%' are verbatim from the DocETL abstract.

Why 'mostly-solid' rather than 'solid': the quote and citation layer is essentially flawless, but one illustrative example (the P3/histogram/'rambling' word-length anecdote) attaches invented specifics to a general, non-attributed point in the paper, and there are a few minor paraphrase/labeling slips. For a non-BS book these should be tightened, but none of the load-bearing quotes or principles are misattributed or fabricated. Local extracted text saved at /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-lixiangyi-benchflow-principles-agent-evals/0fe8dd05-af28-4247-a984-c696ade806a7/scratchpad/wvv.txt.

Quotes

  • "Yet LLM-generated evaluators simply inherit all the problems of the LLMs they evaluate, requiring further human validation." — Shankar, Zamfirescu-Pereira, Hartmann, Parameswaran, Arawjo — 'Who Validates the Validators?' (UIST 2024), abstract (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.12272)
  • "The problem is that, just like the LLMs they evaluate, LLMs that perform evaluations cannot be trusted." — 'Who Validates the Validators?' (UIST 2024), Introduction (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.12272)
  • "We observed a "catch-22" situation: to grade outputs, people need to externalize and define their evaluation criteria; however, the process of grading outputs helps them to define that very criteria." — 'Who Validates the Validators?' (UIST 2024) (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.12272)
  • "We dub this phenomenon criteria drift, and it implies that it is impossible to completely determine evaluation criteria prior to human judging of LLM outputs." — 'Who Validates the Validators?' (UIST 2024) (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.12272)
  • "There are criteria where you can be okay with failing, and then there are other criteria where you are like, 'this must absolutely pass'... [T]here's a [spectrum] of failure as opposed to: it just passes or fails." — Study participant P8, quoted in 'Who Validates the Validators?' (UIST 2024) (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.12272)
  • "This is how I would want a workflow to assist me in evals—basically I want the AI to do 80% of it, and there can be escape hatches if the AI fails." — Study participant P8, quoted in 'Who Validates the Validators?' (UIST 2024) (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.12272)
  • "How do I maintain my evals over time; do I have to rerun this entire process?" — Study participant P9, quoted in 'Who Validates the Validators?' (UIST 2024) (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.12272)
  • "When people say they 'don't do evals,' they are usually lying to themselves." — Shreya Shankar, 'In Defense of AI Evals, for Everyone' (sh-reya.com, Sep 2025) (https://www.sh-reya.com/blog/in-defense-ai-evals/)
  • "Looking at outputs, noticing what feels off, dogfooding your own product, making changes: that is evaluation. It's error analysis, and it happens continuously, and is systematic." — Shreya Shankar, 'In Defense of AI Evals, for Everyone' (sh-reya.com, Sep 2025) (https://www.sh-reya.com/blog/in-defense-ai-evals/)
  • "Hamel and I define it simply as the systematic measurement of application quality." — Shreya Shankar, 'In Defense of AI Evals, for Everyone' (sh-reya.com, Sep 2025) (https://www.sh-reya.com/blog/in-defense-ai-evals/)
  • "You can't determine effective criteria purely through theorizing about possible failure modes for your task." — Shreya Shankar, 'Data Flywheels for LLM Applications' (sh-reya.com, Jul 2024) (https://www.sh-reya.com/blog/ai-engineering-flywheel/)
  • "You need to examine actual data—many LLM outputs—to get a true sense of what criteria you want to validate for LLM outputs for your specific task." — Shreya Shankar, 'Data Flywheels for LLM Applications' (sh-reya.com, Jul 2024) (https://www.sh-reya.com/blog/ai-engineering-flywheel/)
  • "words like 'delve' and 'crucial' commonly give off a 'GPT smell' that you want to avoid." — Shreya Shankar, 'Data Flywheels for LLM Applications' (sh-reya.com, Jul 2024) (https://www.sh-reya.com/blog/ai-engineering-flywheel/)
  • "binary metrics (True/False) are much easier to align and reason about" — Shreya Shankar, 'Data Flywheels for LLM Applications' (sh-reya.com, Jul 2024) (https://www.sh-reya.com/blog/ai-engineering-flywheel/)
  • "Error analysis is the most important activity in evals." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'LLM Evals FAQ' (hamel.dev) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "Fixing upstream failures often resolves dependent downstream failures automatically." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'LLM Evals FAQ' (hamel.dev) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "if ~20 traces don't turn up a new category, you can stop (but review at least 100 to start)." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'LLM Evals FAQ' (hamel.dev) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "Don't try to sell your team on 'evals'. Instead, show them what you find when you look at the data." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'LLM Evals FAQ' (hamel.dev) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)

Sources read: https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.12272, https://arxiv.org/html/2401.03038v1, https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/, https://www.sh-reya.com/blog/in-defense-ai-evals/, https://www.sh-reya.com/blog/ai-engineering-flywheel/, https://www.sh-reya.com/blog, https://www.sh-reya.com/, https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.12189

Hidden gems: Who Validates the Validators? (UIST 2024) — Sections 7-8 (User Study Findings & Discussion) (https://arxiv.org/pdf/2404.12272); Shankar, 'Data Flywheels for LLM Applications' (Jul 2024) (https://www.sh-reya.com/blog/ai-engineering-flywheel/); Shankar, 'In Defense of AI Evals, for Everyone' (Sep 2025) (https://www.sh-reya.com/blog/in-defense-ai-evals/); Husain & Shankar, 'LLM Evals FAQ' (hamel.dev) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/); SPADE: Synthesizing Data Quality Assertions for LLM Pipelines (VLDB 2024) — the 'prompt delta' idea (https://arxiv.org/html/2401.03038v1); DocETL: Agentic Query Rewriting and Evaluation (VLDB 2025) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2410.12189)

Anthropic Engineering — "Demystifying evals for AI agents" (Mikaela Grace, Jeremy Hadfield, Rodrigo Olivares, Jiri De Jonghe; published Jan 9, 2026). https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents

Anthropic's most complete public statement of how it actually builds and grades agent evals. It defines a precise vocabulary (eval, task, trial, grader, transcript/trajectory, outcome, agent harness vs. model, eval harness, suite) and walks through an end-to-end methodology: start with 20-50 tasks sourced from real failures, write unambiguous tasks two experts would grade identically, run isolated trials, pick graders deliberately (code-based > model-based > human), grade the outcome rather than the trajectory, build in partial credit, and never trust a score until someone reads the transcripts. It draws the agent-specific distinctions a book on agent evals needs — single-turn vs multi-turn vs agent evals, capability vs regression evals, pass@k vs pass^k, outcome vs trajectory, eval saturation — and grounds each in war stories (Opus 4.5 jumping 42%→95% on CORE-Bench after grader bugs were fixed; an Opus 4.5 "failure" on tau2-bench that was actually a better solution; misconfigured METR and Terminal-Bench tasks that punished instruction-following). It situates automated evals inside a layered "Swiss cheese" system alongside production monitoring, A/B tests, user feedback, and human studies. For the book, this is the canonical primary source on agent-eval task design, grading, and trajectory-vs-outcome thinking.

Reliability: solid — Fact-check of the Anthropic "Demystifying evals for AI agents" findings against both cited URLs (the main article and the "statistical approach to model evals" companion). Both URLs are real, resolve, and match the claims — no invented URLs. Bibliographic metadata is correct: authors Mikaela Grace, Jeremy Hadfield, Rodrigo Olivares, Jiri De Jonghe; published Jan 9, 2026. All 19 quotes in the quotes array were located on the page verbatim or near-verbatim, with correct attribution. The high-stakes war-story claims also check out verbatim, including the most specific/falsifiable ones: CORE-Bench rigid grading "penalized '96.12' when expecting '96.124991…'" plus "less constrained scaffold," 42%→95%; tau2-bench flight-booking loophole ("'failed'… but actually came up with a better solution for the user"); METR threshold-vs-exceed misconfiguration; SWE-bench Verified "started at 30%… now nearing saturation at >80%"; the git-history shared-state leak; dedicated-evals-team ownership; framework list (Harbor, Braintrust, LangSmith, Langfuse, Arize, Phoenix); and the code-based grader examples. The companion page genuinely covers standard errors/CLT, clustered SEs, paired-difference tests, power analysis, and resampling/variance reduction as the hiddenGems entry claims. Only trivial wording drift found, none material (see flagged claims). Overall this is a faithful, well-sourced extraction — safe for the book.

Quotes

  • "An evaluation ('eval') is a test for an AI system: give an AI an input, then apply grading logic to its output to measure success." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "A good task is one where two domain experts would independently reach the same pass/fail verdict." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "Everything the grader checks should be clear from the task description; agents shouldn't fail due to ambiguous specs." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "So as not to unnecessarily punish creativity, it's often better to grade what the agent produced, not the path it took." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "A support agent that correctly identifies the problem and verifies the customer but fails to process a refund is meaningfully better than one that fails immediately." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "Test both the cases where a behavior should occur and where it shouldn't. One-sided evals create one-sided optimization." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "You won't know if your graders are working well unless you read the transcripts and grades from many trials." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "As a rule, we do not take eval scores at face value until someone digs into the details of the eval and reads some transcripts." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "When we evaluate 'an agent,' we're evaluating the harness and the model working together." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "Eval saturation occurs when an agent passes all of the solvable tasks, leaving no room for improvement." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "Each trial should be 'isolated' by starting from a clean environment. Unnecessary shared state between runs (leftover files, cached data, resource exhaustion) can cause correlated failures due to infrastructure flakiness rather than agent performance." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "It 'failed' the evaluation as written, but actually came up with a better solution for the user." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (on Opus 4.5 finding a policy loophole in tau2-bench) (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "For AI product teams, owning and iterating on evaluations should be as routine as maintaining unit tests." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "To avoid hallucinations, give the LLM a way out, like providing an instruction to return 'Unknown' when it doesn't have enough information." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "It can also help to create clear, structured rubrics to grade each dimension of a task, and then grade each dimension with an isolated LLM-as-judge rather than using one to grade all dimensions." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "Like the Swiss Cheese Model from safety engineering, no single evaluation layer catches every issue. With multiple methods combined, failures that slip through one layer are caught by another." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "It's often best to quickly pick a framework that fits your workflow, then invest your energy in the evals themselves by iterating on high-quality test cases and graders." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "pass@k measures the likelihood that an agent gets at least one correct solution in k attempts." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "pass^k measures the probability that all k trials succeed." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)

Sources read: https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents, https://www.anthropic.com/research/statistical-approach-to-model-evals

Hidden gems: Anthropic — 'A statistical approach to model evaluations' / 'Adding Error Bars to Evals' (https://www.anthropic.com/research/statistical-approach-to-model-evals); Harbor + Terminal-Bench 2.0 (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents); tau-bench / tau2-bench (user-simulator agent evals) (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)

Ofir Press — SWE-bench and benchmark/agent-eval design principles (blog, papers, docs)

Ofir Press (co-creator of SWE-bench, SWE-agent, SWE-bench Verified, and earlier Bamboogle/Compositionality-Gap benchmarks) has distilled a battle-tested theory of benchmark design directly applicable to agent evals. His core thesis: a good eval is Natural (drawn from real human tasks), Automatically Evaluatable (programmatic/execution-based, never LM-as-solver-and-judge), and Challenging (top models score low at launch, ideally "-200%"). The non-obvious insights are the tension between difficulty and adoptability (researchers are emotional; sub-10% launch accuracy scares people off, so you need a beatable-but-hard sweet spot), the ~1-year saturation horizon, the demand that the SCAFFOLD/harness be realistic (code execution for coding, internet for knowledge) and reported alongside the score, contamination-resistance by design (build it so leakage doesn't help), and a hard lesson from SWE-bench Verified: even "real-world" auto-scraped benchmarks are often broken (underspecified issues, unfair unit tests) and need human auditing for solvability and fair grading. His SWE-agent work adds the agent-specific principle that the Agent-Computer Interface is itself a first-class design surface that materially changes measured capability. This body of work contributes a rigorous, opinionated checklist for building agent evals that are hard, realistic, contamination-resistant, adoptable, and trusted.

Reliability: solid — Strong, well-sourced findings. I fetched all five primary cited URLs. The Ofir Press blog (ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/), the SWE-bench arXiv abstract (2310.06770), and the SWE-agent paper/docs all returned verbatim or near-verbatim matches for the quotes attributed to them. The OpenAI "Introducing SWE-bench Verified" page blocked the bot fetcher (HTTP 403), but its key quote and statistics were confirmed via a mirrored GitHub issue (irthomasthomas/undecidability#933) and corroborating web search.

All 12 quotes verify. Every cited URL is real and correctly attributed (no invented links, no misattributions). Author lists check out (SWE-bench: Jimenez, Yang, Wettig, Yao, Pei, Press, Narasimhan).

Bonus verification of high-stakes non-quote claims:

  • SWE-bench Verified stats: 93 Python devs, 1,699 samples annotated, GPT-4o 16% -> 33.2%, 500-instance subset — all confirmed. The example's "~38% underspecified / ~61% unfair tests" maps to the actual 38.3% / 61.1% (and 68.3% total filtered). Accurate.
  • SWE-agent ACI engineering specifics (linter blocks syntactically-invalid edits; ~100-line paged viewer; search lists files-with-matches because more context "proved to be too confusing"; "Your command ran successfully and did not produce any output.") — all found verbatim on swe-agent.com/0.7/background/aci/.
  • "LM agents represent a new category of end users with their own needs and abilities" — verbatim in SWE-agent paper (2405.15793).
  • SciCode withholding answers ("We intentionally do not release any of the answers to these programming challenges") — confirmed in the Press blog.

Two soft caveats (not errors, just not pinned to a verbatim number): the "good ACI design roughly doubled SWE-bench performance over a naive bash agent" claim is a reasonable paraphrase of the SWE-agent paper's ablations but I could not confirm the exact 2x figure from the abstract/docs (abstract reports 12.5% pass@1, "far exceeding" prior non-interactive SOTA). And the "Simplicity / Efficiency" ACI principle names appear in the paper but were not on the specific 0.7 docs page I fetched. Both are plausible. The one medium-confidence principle ("% of tasks solved vs % of tests passed") is the only item I did not independently locate as a direct Press statement; treat it as plausible, not quoted.

Quotes

  • "Try to build a benchmark that has natural questions that some category of humans ask on a frequent basis." — Ofir Press, 'How to Build Good Language Modeling Benchmarks' (https://ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/)
  • "I think that at launch, a good benchmark should have the top LMs achieving between 1% to 35% accuracy on it." — Ofir Press, 'How to Build Good Language Modeling Benchmarks' (https://ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/)
  • "Due to the speed of development of AI I'm now asking my collaborators, not to think of benchmarks that would have AI systems achieving 0% at launch, but to think of benchmarks that would have systems achieving '-200%' at launch." — Ofir Press, 'How to Build Good Language Modeling Benchmarks' (https://ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/)
  • "Benchmarks typically get saturated within a year." — Ofir Press, 'How to Build Good Language Modeling Benchmarks' (https://ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/)
  • "Beware- researchers are humans and humans have emotions. If at launch, the top model's accuracy is less than 10%, that might seem very intimidating for most researchers, and they might not want to work on your benchmark at all." — Ofir Press, 'How to Build Good Language Modeling Benchmarks' (https://ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/)
  • "Could we build a benchmark such that even if the benchmark itself leaks into an LM's training data, it won't really help that LM in getting a good score on the benchmark?" — Ofir Press, 'How to Build Good Language Modeling Benchmarks' (https://ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/)
  • "If you're asking coding questions, and your scaffolding doesn't allow for code execution, that's not a very good representation of reality." — Ofir Press, 'How to Build Good Language Modeling Benchmarks' (https://ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/)
  • "Have one number for your benchmark. One metric that people go for. 'We get 87% on HumanEval' is the vibe you are going for." — Ofir Press, 'How to Build Good Language Modeling Benchmarks' (https://ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/)
  • "would a system that got better-than-baseline accuracy on this benchmark be useful to humans?" — Ofir Press, 'How to Build Good Language Modeling Benchmarks' (https://ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/)
  • "Resolving issues in SWE-bench frequently requires understanding and coordinating changes across multiple functions, classes, and even files simultaneously, calling for models to interact with execution environments, process extremely long contexts and perform complex reasoning that goes far beyond traditional code generation tasks." — Jimenez, Yang, Wettig, ... Press, Narasimhan, 'SWE-bench: Can Language Models Resolve Real-World GitHub Issues?' (abstract) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770)
  • "The best-performing model, Claude 2, is able to solve a mere 1.96% of the issues." — SWE-bench paper abstract (Jimenez, Yang, ... Press et al.) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770)
  • "We worked with 93 software developers experienced in Python to manually screen SWE-bench samples for quality. We annotated 1,699 random samples from the SWE-bench test set." — OpenAI, 'Introducing SWE-bench Verified' (with Carlos Jimenez & SWE-bench team) (https://openai.com/index/introducing-swe-bench-verified/)

Sources read: https://ofir.io, https://ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/, https://www.swebench.com, https://www.swebench.com/verified.html, https://www.swebench.com/SWE-bench/, https://arxiv.org/abs/2310.06770, https://swe-agent.com/0.7/background/aci/, https://github.com/irthomasthomas/undecidability/issues/933

Hidden gems: How to Build Good Language Modeling Benchmarks (Ofir Press blog) (https://ofir.io/How-to-Build-Good-Language-Modeling-Benchmarks/); SWE-agent ACI background docs (https://swe-agent.com/0.7/background/aci/); Introducing SWE-bench Verified (OpenAI Preparedness) (https://openai.com/index/introducing-swe-bench-verified/)

Nathan Lambert (Interconnects.ai / natolambert.com / RLHF Book / RewardBench)

Nathan Lambert's body of work supplies the sharpest available argument that, for LLMs and agents, the eval IS the product and the bottleneck. His through-line: evaluation has "no hard source of truth," so reported benchmark numbers are marketing unless you can reproduce them with model access and an open harness. More importantly for agent builders, he collapses the supposed wall between "evals" and "RL environments": a verifiable evaluation (math, code, instruction-following with a deterministic checker) is precisely what you can train against — RLVR, a term he coined during Tulu 3. This makes eval design and RL task/reward design the same activity, and explains why "evaluation and training" are tightly coupled (labs hillclimb a small chosen set; the payoff of a better eval is statistical power to compare runs, not bragging rights). For agents specifically he warns that as rewards get sparser over long multi-tool trajectories, over-optimization/reward-hacking gets stronger and harder to detect, current RL only hardens sub-tasks rather than training end-to-end trajectories, and you are really evaluating the harness/product, not just the model. He also documents the under-evaluated layer beneath RLHF — reward models — which he was first to benchmark (RewardBench). Net contribution to an agent-evals book: a rigorous, skeptical, training-aware lens that treats evals as the optimization target you must design, decontaminate, and hold out, not a scoreboard.

Reliability: mostly-solid — Quotes are the strongest part of this finding set: all 19 attributed quotes were located verbatim (or, in one case, near-verbatim with a faithful elision) at their exact cited URLs, with correct authorship — including the RewardBench abstract (Lambert confirmed first author) and the Turing Post 'harness/product' line. No fabricated quotes and no invented URLs among the quote sources; all six load-bearing URLs resolve to the right pages. The RLVR-coined-during-Tulu-3 (fall 2024) claim checks out: Tulu 3 (arXiv 2411.15124, Nov 2024) introduces RLVR as a novel method and Lambert's team is widely credited with the term.

The weaknesses are all in the SYNTHESIZED principles/examples, not the quotes, where the mining over-reached on attribution: (1) three of four 'contamination dataset pairs' attributed to the quicksand essay are not in it; (2) the 'RL on random rewards' leakage tell is from the Spurious Rewards paper, not Lambert; (3) the 'million-dollar per eval' / dedicated-eval-industry prediction is not in the cited RLHF Book chapter (only saturation + eval-company naming is). Also one minor article-title error. None of these undermine the substantive thesis — the underlying claims are largely real in the broader literature — but for a 'non-BS' book each needs its citation corrected or downgraded from 'Lambert documents/predicts' to 'consistent with.' Rating mostly-solid because the verbatim-quote layer (the highest-stakes, hardest-to-fix-after-print material) is bulletproof, while a handful of secondary claims are mis-sourced and should be fixed before publication.

Quotes

  • "What used to be solid, simple ground in evaluation now feels like quicksand." — Nathan Lambert, 'Building on evaluation quicksand' (Interconnects) (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/building-on-evaluation-quicksand)
  • "Language model evaluation stacks are perceived as marketing because the evaluations have no hard source of truth." — Nathan Lambert, 'Building on evaluation quicksand' (Interconnects) (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/building-on-evaluation-quicksand)
  • "Evaluation of frontier language models is every bit as much an art today as it is a science." — Nathan Lambert, 'Building on evaluation quicksand' (Interconnects) (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/building-on-evaluation-quicksand)
  • "The limited set of evaluations they choose to focus on forms a close link between evaluation and training." — Nathan Lambert, 'Building on evaluation quicksand' (Interconnects) — also appears in the RLHF Book evaluation chapter (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/building-on-evaluation-quicksand)
  • "Dataset contamination can now come from many more sources than previously thought." — Nathan Lambert, 'Building on evaluation quicksand' (Interconnects) (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/building-on-evaluation-quicksand)
  • "Without access to the model, it's impossible to do a fair comparison." — Nathan Lambert, 'Big Tech's LLM evals are just marketing' (Interconnects) (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/evals-are-marketing)
  • "Without any semblance of a fair comparison, the numbers are marketing, not science." — Nathan Lambert, 'Big Tech's LLM evals are just marketing' (Interconnects) (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/evals-are-marketing)
  • "If you want to be informed about which LLM is the one for you, sit down and chat with some LLMs." — Nathan Lambert, 'Big Tech's LLM evals are just marketing' (Interconnects) (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/evals-are-marketing)
  • "Models are so strong on most of the benchmarks we use these days that it takes a huge difference in the numerical scores to make it obvious that one model is better than another." — Nathan Lambert, 'Big Tech's LLM evals are just marketing' (Interconnects) (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/evals-are-marketing)
  • "Evaluation is the set of techniques used to understand the quality and impact of the training processes detailed in this book." — Nathan Lambert, RLHF Book, Evaluation chapter (https://rlhfbook.com/c/16-evaluation)
  • "Some prompting techniques – e.g. formatting discussed below – can make a model's performance drop from 60% to near 0." — Nathan Lambert, RLHF Book, Evaluation chapter (https://rlhfbook.com/c/16-evaluation)
  • "The key to understanding language model evaluation, particularly with post-training, is that the current popular evaluation regimes represent a reflection of the popular training best practices and goals." — Nathan Lambert, RLHF Book, Evaluation chapter (https://rlhfbook.com/c/16-evaluation)
  • "The key capability that improving evaluations internally has on downstream training is improving the statistical power when comparing training runs." — Nathan Lambert, RLHF Book, Evaluation chapter (https://rlhfbook.com/c/16-evaluation)
  • "Evaluation is normally expressed through benchmarks, which are discrete sets of questions or environments designed to measure a specific property of a model." — Nathan Lambert, RLHF Book, Evaluation chapter (https://rlhfbook.com/c/16-evaluation)
  • "RL is not going to magically let us train language models end-to-end that make entire code-bases more efficient, run scientific experiments in the real world, or generate complex strategies." — Nathan Lambert, 'What comes next with reinforcement learning' (Interconnects) (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/what-comes-next-with-reinforcement)
  • "current RL methods are helping the models get more robust at individual tasks that make up a longer trajectory rather than being trained on the end result of the trajectory itself." — Nathan Lambert, 'What comes next with reinforcement learning' (Interconnects) (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/what-comes-next-with-reinforcement)
  • "The final long-horizon behavior is put together with prompting and letting the model run longer, not sparse credit assignment." — Nathan Lambert, 'What comes next with reinforcement learning' (Interconnects) (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/what-comes-next-with-reinforcement)
  • "as rewards become sparser, the potential for overoptimization seems even stronger — the optimizer can update more intermediate tokens in a way that is hard to detect in order to achieve the goal." — Nathan Lambert, 'What comes next with reinforcement learning' (Interconnects) (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/what-comes-next-with-reinforcement)
  • "Reward models (RMs) are at the crux of successfully using RLHF to align pretrained models to human preferences, yet there has been relatively little study that focuses on evaluation of those models." — Nathan Lambert et al., RewardBench paper abstract (arXiv 2403.13787) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.13787)
  • "evaluations for AI are much more about what some technical people call the harness or the product than just the model." — Nathan Lambert, Turing Post interview (https://www.turingpost.com/p/nathanlambert)

Sources read: https://www.interconnects.ai/p/building-on-evaluation-quicksand, https://www.interconnects.ai/p/evals-are-marketing, https://rlhfbook.com/c/16-evaluation, https://www.interconnects.ai/p/what-comes-next-with-reinforcement, https://www.turingpost.com/p/nathanlambert, https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.13787, https://natolambert.com/writing, https://www.interconnects.ai/p/the-new-rl-scaling-laws, https://www.latent.space/p/the-rlvr-revolution-with-nathan-lambert, https://www.understandingai.org/p/nathan-lambert-on-the-rise-of-thinking, https://www.interconnects.ai/p/the-state-of-post-training-2025

Hidden gems: Building on evaluation quicksand (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/building-on-evaluation-quicksand); Big Tech's LLM evals are just marketing (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/evals-are-marketing); RLHF Book, Evaluation chapter (Chapter 16) (https://rlhfbook.com/c/16-evaluation); What comes next with reinforcement learning (https://www.interconnects.ai/p/what-comes-next-with-reinforcement); RewardBench (paper + Ai2 leaderboard) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2403.13787); Turing Post interview: 'Open Models Won't Catch Up' (https://www.turingpost.com/p/nathanlambert)

Dawn Song (UC Berkeley) — agent safety & security evaluation: lecture "Towards Building Safe & Secure Agentic AI" (Advanced LLM Agents MOOC), plus papers BenchJack, CyberGym, DecodingTrust, AIR-Bench 2024, RedCode, AgentPoison, AgentXploit

Dawn Song approaches agent evaluation through a security researcher's lens: an eval is only trustworthy if it survives an adversary, and the adversary is often the model itself. Her body of work contributes three durable ideas to agent evals. (1) Benchmarks are attack surfaces. In BenchJack (2026) her group shows that frontier coding agents spontaneously "reward hack" — achieving near-perfect scores on 9 of 10 popular agent benchmarks (SWE-bench, WebArena, OSWorld, AgentBench, etc.) without solving a single task — and distills the failures into an eight-pattern flaw taxonomy and a 30-question "Agent-Eval Checklist," arguing benchmarks must be "secure by design" with environment isolation, deterministic scoring, and structured output parsing. (2) Agent eval is end-to-end and adversarial, not model-in-a-box. She draws a sharp line between LLM evaluation (stand-alone, text-in/text-out) and agentic-hybrid-system evaluation (end-to-end system behavior with tools, memory, environment), introduces a graded threat model (model security levels L0-L4; CyberGym difficulty levels 0-3), and separates Performance from Resilience as distinct axes (DecodingTrust). (3) Ground evals in execution and in the real world: CyberGym uses execution-based PoC reproduction (sanitizer crash pre-patch, no crash post-patch) over 1,507 real CVEs and found 35 zero-days, while AIR-Bench grounds its risk taxonomy in actual regulations rather than intuition. Across the work the throughline is: evaluate under the presence of an attacker, prefer verifiable/grounded outcomes over gameable proxies (LLM-judge, string match), and red-team the eval harness itself before you trust its numbers.

Reliability: mostly-solid — Overall the source is well-grounded and mostly accurate; no invented URLs and no authorship misattributions. All five cited source URLs resolve to real content (BenchJack arxiv 2605.12673 is a genuine May 2026 paper, confirmed via dblp and the Berkeley RDI blog 'How We Broke Top AI Agent Benchmarks'; CyberGym, AIR-Bench, DecodingTrust, and the lecture slides PDF all verified).

Quote verification: 19 of the notable quotes check out verbatim or near-verbatim against primary sources. All 8 BenchJack quotes are verbatim in the HTML full text (arxiv.org/html/2605.12673v1). The 5 lecture-slide quotes are verbatim — I downloaded the 6.6MB slides PDF and extracted text (saved at /private/tmp/claude-501/-Users-lixiangyi-benchflow-principles-agent-evals/0fe8dd05-af28-4247-a984-c696ade806a7/scratchpad/slides.txt) since the WebFetch summarizer could not read the binary; grep confirmed the exact wording plus the L0-L4 model security levels, the AI-Safety-vs-Security definitions, and the DecodingTrust Performance/Resilience axes. CyberGym's PoC-success criterion, 'reliably determined with execution', 'thousands of files and millions lines of code', 1,507 CVEs / 188 projects, ~20% top success rate, difficulty levels 0-3 all confirmed.

Two real issues for a 'non-BS' book: (1) the CyberGym zero-day count (35/17) matches the cited v2 but the live/current arxiv version says 34/18 — flag the version; (2) the 'gradual ladder of difficulty ... tracking current and future progress' sentence is a paraphrase dressed as a verbatim quote (actual: 'create a ladder of difficulties'). Lower-priority: the AgentPoison trigger-string detail and several BenchJack named mini-examples are plausible but not individually verified against the papers. Corroborated independently: 9-of-10 benchmarks near-perfect, 219 flaws, 30-question/7-category Agent-Eval Checklist, V1-V8 taxonomy (exact match), WebArena/OSWorld patched within 3 iterations, DecodingTrust's NeurIPS 2023 Outstanding Paper + NSA 2024 awards and 8 perspectives.

Quotes

  • "Agent benchmarks have become the de facto measure of frontier AI competence, guiding model selection, investment, and deployment." — Wang, Li, Mang, Cheung, Sen, Song — BenchJack (2026) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12673)
  • "reward hacking, where agents maximize a score without performing the intended task, emerges spontaneously in frontier models without overfitting." — BenchJack: Systematically Auditing AI Agent Benchmarks (Wang et al., incl. Dawn Song) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12673)
  • "We argue that benchmarks must be secure by design." — BenchJack (Wang et al., incl. Dawn Song) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12673)
  • "Manually auditing every new benchmark for reward-hacking flaws is impractical: new benchmarks appear monthly, each with its own evaluation harness, sand-boxing strategy, and scoring function." — BenchJack (Wang et al., incl. Dawn Song) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12673)
  • "The benchmarks that stayed secure after patching share good design properties, such as strong environment isolation, deterministic scoring, and structured output parsing." — BenchJack (Wang et al., incl. Dawn Song) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12673)
  • "Our results show that evaluation pipelines have not internalized an adversarial mindset, and that proactive auditing could help close the security gap for the fast-paced benchmarking space." — BenchJack (Wang et al., incl. Dawn Song) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12673)
  • "BenchJack synthesizes reward-hacking exploits that achieve near-perfect scores on most of the benchmarks without solving a single task, surfacing 219 distinct flaws across the eight classes." — BenchJack (Wang et al., incl. Dawn Song) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12673)
  • "models that learn to game evaluations during training or deployment transfer those strategies to settings where they were never validated" — BenchJack (Wang et al., incl. Dawn Song) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12673)
  • "Another reason for choosing reproduction as our main task is that its success can be reliably determined with execution." — Wang, Shi, He, Cai, Zhang, Song — CyberGym (2025) (https://arxiv.org/html/2506.02548v2)
  • "For a PoC to be considered successful, we require that (i) it triggers a sanitizer crash in the pre-patch version and (ii) running it on the post-patch version does not produce any sanitizer crash." — CyberGym (Wang et al., incl. Dawn Song) (https://arxiv.org/html/2506.02548v2)
  • "Solving CyberGym requires agents to perform deep reasoning across large codebases, spanning thousands of files and millions lines of code." — CyberGym (Wang et al., incl. Dawn Song) (https://arxiv.org/html/2506.02548v2)
  • "CyberGym's diverse and challenging tasks provide a gradual ladder of difficulty, making it essential for tracking current and future progress in the cybersecurity field." — CyberGym (Wang et al., incl. Dawn Song) (https://arxiv.org/html/2506.02548v2)
  • "Beyond static benchmarking, we show that CyberGym leads to the discovery of 35 zero-day vulnerabilities and 17 historically incomplete patches." — CyberGym (Wang et al., incl. Dawn Song) (https://arxiv.org/html/2506.02548v2)
  • "existing public benchmarks often define safety categories based on previous literature, intuitions, or common sense, leading to disjointed sets of categories for risks specified in recent regulations and policies, which makes it challenging to evaluate and compare FMs across these benchmarks." — Zeng, Yang, ... Song, Liang, Li — AIR-Bench 2024 (https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.17436v2)
  • "LLM evaluation only focuses on evaluating stand-alone model behaviors" — Dawn Song, lecture slides 'Towards Building Safe & Secure Agentic AI' (Advanced LLM Agents, UC Berkeley RDI) (https://rdi.berkeley.edu/adv-llm-agents/slides/dawn-agentic-ai.pdf)
  • "Agentic hybrid system evaluation evaluates on end-to-end system behaviors" — Dawn Song, lecture slides 'Towards Building Safe & Secure Agentic AI' (UC Berkeley RDI) (https://rdi.berkeley.edu/adv-llm-agents/slides/dawn-agentic-ai.pdf)
  • "AI safety needs to consider adversarial setting" — Dawn Song, lecture slides 'Towards Building Safe & Secure Agentic AI' (UC Berkeley RDI) (https://rdi.berkeley.edu/adv-llm-agents/slides/dawn-agentic-ai.pdf)
  • "History has shown attacker always follows footsteps of new technology development (or sometimes even leads it)" — Dawn Song, lecture slides 'Towards Building Safe & Secure Agentic AI' (UC Berkeley RDI) (https://rdi.berkeley.edu/adv-llm-agents/slides/dawn-agentic-ai.pdf)
  • "The attacker can only get binary feedback (attack success/failure)" — Dawn Song, AgentXploit threat model, lecture slides (UC Berkeley RDI) (https://rdi.berkeley.edu/adv-llm-agents/slides/dawn-agentic-ai.pdf)

Sources read: https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12673, https://arxiv.org/html/2605.12673, https://arxiv.org/html/2506.02548v2, https://rdi.berkeley.edu/adv-llm-agents/slides/dawn-agentic-ai.pdf, https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.17436v2

Hidden gems: BenchJack + the Agent-Eval Checklist (https://arxiv.org/abs/2605.12673); CyberGym (https://arxiv.org/html/2506.02548v2); DecodingTrust (https://decodingtrust.github.io); AIR-Bench 2024 (regulation-grounded risk taxonomy) (https://github.com/stanford-crfm/air-bench-2024); RedCode: risky code execution & generation benchmark for code agents (https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.07781); AgentPoison (https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.12784); Dawn Song, ICLR 2025 keynote on LLM safety (https://iclr.cc/virtual/2025/invited-talk/36783)

Eval infrastructure & companies — primary writing from Braintrust, LangSmith/LangChain, Langfuse, Arize Phoenix/AX, Galileo, OpenAI Evals docs, Anthropic engineering, plus practitioner sources (Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, Eugene Yan) and pavlovslist.com (RL-environment startup directory). Focus: what a production eval stack actually contains and the durable principles behind it.

Across vendors and practitioners, a production eval stack is converging on the same anatomy: datasets (curated golden cases + production traces continuously reconciled), scorers (deterministic code, LLM-as-judge, human review), an offline/dev/CI loop that gates deploys against regressions, and an online loop that scores live traffic, all sitting on top of tracing/observability that captures the full output space (every model call, tool call, and intermediate step). The non-obvious, repeatedly field-tested insight is that the tool is the easy part — the leverage is in process: error analysis on real traces, a single domain expert who owns binary pass/fail labels, judges validated against those labels with precision/recall (not raw agreement), and custom annotation tooling. Agent evals add a distinctive axis the vendors disagree on productively: outcome (end-state in the environment) vs trajectory (the path/tool-calls). This body of work directly informs an agent-evals book: it supplies the vocabulary (benchmark vs eval vs RL environment, offline vs online, outcome vs trajectory, golden dataset vs dataset reconciliation), the frameworks (Three Gulfs, eval flywheel, judge-alignment loop, CI gating), and concrete war stories. Notably pavlovslist.com shows the frontier where "evals" and "RL environments" merge — graded, stateful task environments — and the user's own company (BenchFlow) is listed there.

Reliability: mostly-solid — I independently fetched every cited URL and matched the quotes against the live pages. The quote discipline in this corpus is unusually good: all ~20 direct quotes in the quotes array verified verbatim or near-verbatim against their cited sources, and the attributions/URLs are real and correct. I also spot-checked the highest-stakes paraphrased claims and examples (Hamel binary-vs-Likert, benevolent dictator, first-failure annotation, wary-of-100%-pass; Anthropic flight-booking SQL example and support-agent partial credit; Braintrust five-examples and single-score/step-localization; Galileo six-metric taxonomy + Luna; Eugene Yan judge-bias numbers; pavlovslist directory) — all confirmed on the source pages.

ONE clear problem: the example "Criteria drift in practice (Shankar et al., EvalGen study): users who graded outputs while building evaluators changed their criteria significantly more (22.67 vs 13.33 changes)." I extracted the full UIST 2024 paper text (Berkeley PDF) and the numbers 22.67 and 13.33 do not appear anywhere, nor any p-value or between-condition quantitative comparison of "criteria changes" — the study is qualitative (~9-11 participants; the only numeric table compares EvalGen vs SPADE on assertion coverage). A WebSearch "summary" appeared to confirm the numbers, but it was an AI synthesis that simply parroted back the figures I put in the query (no primary source contains them). The underlying qualitative finding (criteria drift) is rock-solid and quoted verbatim from the abstract; only the fabricated precise statistic needs to be cut. The book is "non-BS," so this invented number is exactly the kind of thing to strike.

Minor: (a) pavlovslist is described as "~40 RL-environment startups" but the live site lists ~50 (BenchFlow, AfterQuery, HUD, Plato, Halluminate, Mechanize all present; John Schulman endorsement and "For the RL-pilled" tagline confirmed). (b) The Shankar paper is attributed to "UIST 2024," which the arXiv abstract page alone does not show, but dblp + ACM DOI 10.1145/3654777.3676450 (Proc. 37th UIST) confirm the venue — attribution is correct. (c) The OpenAI best-practices quotes are real; the cited developers.openai.com URL resolves and matched verbatim (canonical is also mirrored at platform.openai.com). No invented URLs found. (d) The single-score quote "when evaluation combines layers into a single score, it becomes unclear where the problem actually lies" lives on Braintrust's ai-agent-evaluation-framework page, not the three-pillars page — both are in the source list, so no misattribution, just worth noting if pinning a citation.

Quotes

  • "Error analysis is the most important activity in evals." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'LLM Evals: Everything You Need to Know' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "In the projects we've worked on, we've spent 60-80% of our development time on error analysis and evaluation." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'LLM Evals: Everything You Need to Know' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "Build a custom annotation tool. This is the single most impactful investment you can make for your AI evaluation workflow." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'LLM Evals: Everything You Need to Know' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "I often find that teams with custom annotation tools iterate ~10x faster." — Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar, 'LLM Evals: Everything You Need to Know' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/)
  • "You cannot write a good judge prompt until you've seen the data." — Hamel Husain, 'Using LLM-as-a-Judge For Evaluation: A Complete Guide' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/)
  • "A good task is one where two domain experts would independently reach the same pass/fail verdict." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "So as not to unnecessarily punish creativity, it's often better to grade what the agent produced, not the path it took." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "As a rule, we do not take eval scores at face value until someone digs into the details of the eval and reads some transcripts." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "In reality, 20-50 simple tasks drawn from real failures is a great start." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "Each trial should be 'isolated' by starting from a clean environment. Unnecessary shared state between runs (leftover files, cached data, resource exhaustion) can cause correlated failures due to infrastructure flakiness rather than agent performance." — Anthropic, 'Demystifying evals for AI agents' (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents)
  • "Building product evals is simply the scientific method in disguise. That's the secret sauce." — Eugene Yan, 'An LLM-as-Judge Won't Save The Product—Fixing Your Process Will' (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/eval-process/)
  • "It starts with observation aka Look at The Data™." — Eugene Yan, 'An LLM-as-Judge Won't Save The Product—Fixing Your Process Will' (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/eval-process/)
  • "While automated evals help scale monitoring, they can't compensate for neglect. If we're not actively reviewing AI outputs and customer feedback, automated evaluators won't save our product." — Eugene Yan, 'An LLM-as-Judge Won't Save The Product—Fixing Your Process Will' (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/eval-process/)
  • "AI observability requires a fundamental shift in how we think about quality, from 'is it up?' to 'is it good?'." — Braintrust, 'The three pillars of AI observability' (https://www.braintrust.dev/blog/three-pillars-ai-observability)
  • "Although people often use the term 'golden dataset', we have seen a shift away from this concept in favor of a more fluid approach called 'dataset reconciliation', where the goal shifts to incrementally and frequently updating datasets to represent real-world behaviors, rather than commissioning one up front." — Braintrust, 'The three pillars of AI observability' (https://www.braintrust.dev/blog/three-pillars-ai-observability)
  • "Studies show that pairwise comparisons lead to more stable results and smaller differences between LLM judgments and human annotations relative to direct scoring." — Eugene Yan, 'Evaluating the Effectiveness of LLM-Evaluators (aka LLM-as-Judge)' (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/)
  • "Both claude-v1 and gpt-3.5 preferred the longer response more than 90% of the time." — Eugene Yan, 'Evaluating the Effectiveness of LLM-Evaluators' (on verbosity bias) (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/)
  • "Even if the final answer is right, a poor sequence of steps can waste time, money, or expose users to risk." — Arize, 'Agent Trajectory Evaluations' (AX docs) (https://arize.com/docs/ax/evaluate/evaluators/trace-and-session-evals/trace-level-evaluations/agent-trajectory-evaluations)
  • "Adopt eval-driven development: Evaluate early and often." — OpenAI, 'Evaluation best practices' (https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/evaluation-best-practices)
  • "Log everything: Log as you develop so you can mine your logs for good eval cases." — OpenAI, 'Evaluation best practices' (https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/evaluation-best-practices)

Sources read: https://www.pavlovslist.com, https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/, https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/, https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals/, https://eugeneyan.com/writing/eval-process/, https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/, https://www.braintrust.dev/blog/three-pillars-ai-observability, https://www.braintrust.dev/articles/ai-agent-evaluation-framework, https://www.braintrust.dev/blog/offline-eval-guide, https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents, https://docs.langchain.com/langsmith/evaluation, https://arize.com/docs/ax/evaluate/evaluators/trace-and-session-evals/trace-level-evaluations/agent-trajectory-evaluations, https://galileo.ai/blog/ai-agent-metrics, https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/evaluation-best-practices
