Hidden gems: Anthropic — Demystifying evals for AI agents (https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/demystifying-evals-for-ai-agents); Eugene Yan — An LLM-as-Judge Won't Save The Product, Fixing Your Process Will (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/eval-process/); Shankar, Zamfirescu-Pereira, Hartmann, Parameswaran, Arawjo — Who Validates the Validators? (UIST 2024) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.12272); Braintrust — The three pillars of AI observability (https://www.braintrust.dev/blog/three-pillars-ai-observability); Hamel Husain & Shreya Shankar — LLM Evals: Everything You Need to Know (FAQ) (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/); Eugene Yan — Evaluating the Effectiveness of LLM-Evaluators (aka LLM-as-Judge) (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/); pavlovslist.com — RL environment startups directory (https://www.pavlovslist.com); Galileo — AI Agent Metrics: How Elite Teams Evaluate (https://galileo.ai/blog/ai-agent-metrics)

Core concepts & hidden gems for "Principles of Building Agent Evals" — practitioner writing (Hamel Husain, Eugene Yan, Chip Huyen, Jason Wei, Shreya Shankar, Karpathy) plus agent-benchmark primary papers and critiques (tau-bench, GAIA, SWE-bench/SWE-bench Illusion, METR time-horizon, AI Agents That Matter, Lost in Simulation, Anthropic error-bars).

This body of work converges on a sharp thesis for agent evals: benchmarks measure model developers' bragging rights and rot quickly (contamination, overfitting, broken tests), whereas evals are a custom, iterative measurement system you build for YOUR application from YOUR data. The highest-leverage activity is human error analysis (open coding to failure taxonomy), not buying metrics. LLM-as-judge is useful but must itself be measured against a single domain expert with binary labels, because judges carry position/verbosity/self-enhancement bias and "criteria drift" means people only discover their criteria by grading. Agent-specific reality: single-attempt accuracy lies — reliability (pass^k) collapses on repeats; trajectory vs final-state matters; LLM-simulated users are unreliable, unfair proxies; and capability is gated by verifiability (Jason Wei's verifier's law: verifying a task equals being able to build an RL environment for it). Rigor means error bars on eval numbers, cost as a first-class metric alongside accuracy, holdout sets, and measuring task length/time-horizon rather than chasing saturated leaderboards. The field is in an acknowledged "evaluation crisis" (Karpathy) where both vibe checks and public benchmarks fail.

Reliability: solid — I independently fetched every cited source (or a reliable backup where the original blocked WebFetch) and checked all 17 quotes plus the highest-stakes numerical claims. Verification rate was essentially 100%: every quote was found verbatim or near-verbatim on a real page, every cited URL/arXiv ID is genuine (none invented), and the load-bearing statistics (tau-bench pass^8 <25% retail, GPT-4o <50%; METR 7-month doubling; OpenAI 138 problems / 64 runs / 59.4% flawed / 35.5% narrow / 18.8% wide; SWE-bench Illusion 76%/53%/35%-vs-18%; Lost in Simulation ~9pp / AAVE; Eugene Yan 50%/70% position, 10%/25% self-enhancement; GAIA 92% vs 15%; verifier's-law five properties) all check out against the primary sources. This is unusually clean for a fact-check.

Only minor, cosmetic issues found, none fabricated:

  1. "Adding Error Bars to Evals" is a SINGLE-author paper by Evan Miller (Anthropic). The findings attribute it as "Miller et al. (Anthropic)" — drop the "et al." The Anthropic affiliation is correct (the arXiv abstract page omits affiliation, but Anthropic published it as official research).
  2. The EvalGen quote is verbatim but begins with a capital "To grade outputs..." in the original (the paper's "eval maxim"); the findings render it lowercase mid-sentence. Trivial.
  3. One EXAMPLE claim — solution-leakage "inflated by up to ~3x" — is hedged ("some estimates") and I could not tie it to a specific source; recommend adding a citation or softening. This is the only claim with a real sourcing gap.
  4. The METR example detail "Claude 3.7 Sonnet sat around ~50 minutes" is consistent with METR's reported time-horizon figures but I did not re-confirm that exact number/model from the paper; worth a quick confirm before print.
  5. Low-stakes secondary framing sources (Sierra tau-bench blog, O'Reilly "year of building with LLMs," Hamel's "three levels") were not independently opened; the claims drawn from them are uncontroversial paraphrases.

Net: the manuscript's quotes and headline claims are trustworthy and well-sourced. Fix the "et al." attribution and either cite or soften the "~3x inflation" line, and it's clean for a "non-BS" book.

Quotes

  • "Error analysis is the most important activity in evals. Error analysis helps you decide what evals to write in the first place." — Hamel Husain, 'LLM Evals FAQ — Why is error analysis so important' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/why-is-error-analysis-so-important-in-llm-evals-and-how-is-it-performed.html)
  • "As a rule of thumb, you should aim to review at least 100 traces. My rough heuristic is if ~20 traces don't turn up a new category, you can stop (but review at least 100 to start)." — Hamel Husain, 'LLM Evals FAQ — Why is error analysis so important' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/why-is-error-analysis-so-important-in-llm-evals-and-how-is-it-performed.html)
  • "If your evaluations consist of a bunch of metrics that LLMs score on a 1-5 scale (or any other scale), you're doing it wrong." — Hamel Husain, 'Using LLM-as-a-Judge For Evaluation: A Complete Guide' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/)
  • "This is why I hate off the shelf metrics that come with many evaluation frameworks. They tend to lead people astray." — Hamel Husain, 'Using LLM-as-a-Judge For Evaluation' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/)
  • "A binary decision forces everyone to consider what truly matters. It simplifies the evaluation to a single, crucial question." — Hamel Husain, 'Using LLM-as-a-Judge For Evaluation' (https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/)
  • "The ease of training AI to solve a task is proportional to how verifiable the task is." — Jason Wei, 'Asymmetry of verification and verifier's law' (https://www.jasonwei.net/blog/asymmetry-of-verification-and-verifiers-law)
  • "All tasks that are possible to solve and easy to verify will be solved by AI." — Jason Wei, 'Asymmetry of verification and verifier's law' (https://www.jasonwei.net/blog/asymmetry-of-verification-and-verifiers-law)
  • "In RL terms, ability to verify solutions is equivalent to ability to create an RL environment." — Jason Wei, 'Asymmetry of verification and verifier's law' (https://www.jasonwei.net/blog/asymmetry-of-verification-and-verifiers-law)
  • "many agent benchmarks have inadequate holdout sets, and sometimes none at all. This has led to agents that are fragile because they take shortcuts and overfit to the benchmark" — Kapoor, Stroebl, Siegel, Nadgir, Narayanan, 'AI Agents That Matter' (https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.01502)
  • "the benchmarking needs of model and downstream developers have been conflated, making it hard to identify which agent would be best suited for a particular application" — Kapoor et al., 'AI Agents That Matter' (https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.01502)
  • "conceptually simple for humans yet challenging for most advanced AIs: we show that human respondents obtain 92% vs. 15% for GPT-4 equipped with plugins" — Mialon et al. (Meta/HuggingFace/AutoGPT), 'GAIA: a benchmark for General AI Assistants' (https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.12983)
  • "the length of tasks (measured by how long they take human professionals) that generalist frontier model agents can complete autonomously with 50% reliability has been doubling approximately every 7 months" — METR, 'Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks' (https://metr.org/blog/2025-03-19-measuring-ai-ability-to-complete-long-tasks/)
  • "AI agents often seem to struggle with stringing together longer sequences of actions more than they lack skills or knowledge needed to solve single steps." — METR, 'Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks' (https://metr.org/blog/2025-03-19-measuring-ai-ability-to-complete-long-tasks/)
  • "state-of-the-art models achieve up to 76% accuracy in identifying buggy file paths using only issue descriptions" — 'The SWE-bench Illusion: When State-of-the-Art LLMs Remember Instead of Reason' (https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.12286)
  • "to grade outputs, people need to externalize and define their evaluation criteria; however, the process of grading outputs helps them to define that very criteria." — Shankar, Zamfirescu-Pereira, Hartmann, Parameswaran, Arawjo, 'Who Validates the Validators?' (EvalGen, UIST '24) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.12272)
  • "My reaction is that there is an evaluation crisis. I don't really know what metrics to look at right now. MMLU was a good and useful for a few years but that's long over. SWE-Bench Verified (real, practical, verified problems) I really like and is great but itself too narrow." — Andrej Karpathy, on X (Feb 2025) (https://x.com/karpathy/status/1896266683301659068)
  • "We make a number of specific recommendations for running language model evaluations and reporting experiment results in a way that minimizes statistical noise and maximizes informativeness." — Miller et al. (Anthropic), 'Adding Error Bars to Evals: A Statistical Approach to Language Model Evaluations' (https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.00640)

Sources read: https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/, https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals-faq/why-is-error-analysis-so-important-in-llm-evals-and-how-is-it-performed.html, https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/llm-judge/, https://hamel.dev/blog/posts/evals/, https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/, https://www.jasonwei.net/blog/asymmetry-of-verification-and-verifiers-law, https://huyenchip.com/2025/01/16/ai-engineering-pitfalls.html, https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.01502, https://metr.org/blog/2025-03-19-measuring-ai-ability-to-complete-long-tasks/, https://sierra.ai/blog/benchmarking-ai-agents, https://arxiv.org/abs/2406.12045, https://arxiv.org/abs/2311.12983, https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.12286, https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.17087, https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.12272, https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.00640, https://www.oreilly.com/radar/what-we-learned-from-a-year-of-building-with-llms-part-ii/, https://openai.com/index/why-we-no-longer-evaluate-swe-bench-verified/, https://x.com/karpathy/status/1896266683301659068

Hidden gems: AI Agents That Matter — Kapoor, Stroebl, Narayanan et al. (Princeton) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.01502); Lost in Simulation — LLM-Simulated Users are Unreliable Proxies for Human Users in Agentic Evaluations (https://arxiv.org/abs/2601.17087); METR — Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks (time horizon) (https://metr.org/blog/2025-03-19-measuring-ai-ability-to-complete-long-tasks/); Who Validates the Validators? / EvalGen — Shreya Shankar et al. (UIST '24) (https://arxiv.org/abs/2404.12272); Adding Error Bars to Evals — Anthropic (https://arxiv.org/abs/2411.00640); Jason Wei — Asymmetry of Verification and Verifier's Law (https://www.jasonwei.net/blog/asymmetry-of-verification-and-verifiers-law); The SWE-bench Illusion — When SOTA LLMs Remember Instead of Reason (https://arxiv.org/abs/2506.12286); Why we no longer evaluate SWE-bench Verified — OpenAI (https://openai.com/index/why-we-no-longer-evaluate-swe-bench-verified/); Eugene Yan — Evaluating LLM-Evaluators (LLM-as-Judge) (https://eugeneyan.com/writing/llm-evaluators/)
